Option B uses Change Manager for approval, Lambda to temporarily assign the permission set, and a scheduled rule to revoke. This matches all three requirements: elevated access, approval gate, auto-expiry. Option C's tag-based approach doesn't implement approval or expiry. Option D bypasses Identity Center entirely.
questions.pattern: Identity Center permission sets are static assignments. Time-bound or approval-gated access needs orchestration: Change Manager or custom workflow + Lambda to assign/revoke + EventBridge scheduler for expiry. Session duration ≠ access duration — session duration just limits how long ONE login is valid.

references.title: Temporary elevated access with Identity Center

questions.scenario: A company runs a real-time bidding platform on EKS. Latency budget is 80ms end-to-end. Peak traffic is 40,000 requests/second. The platform uses multiple microservices communicating via gRPC. Currently, services discover each other via Kubernetes DNS, but cross-AZ traffic is adding unpredictable latency (10-30ms) and incurring significant data transfer costs. The team wants to reduce cross-AZ hops while maintaining high availability across 3 AZs.
questions.question: Which approach MOST effectively reduces cross-AZ latency and cost without sacrificing HA?
choices: Use AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecars configured for zone-aware routing, preferring pod endpoints in the same AZ but failing over to other AZs if local targets are unhealthy.

Use Kubernetes Topology Aware Hints (or Topology Aware Routing) with EKS, which instructs kube-proxy to prefer endpoints in the same zone. Combine with PodTopologySpreadConstraints to ensure each service has pods in all 3 AZs.
Collapse the cluster to a single AZ to eliminate cross-AZ traffic entirely; accept reduced availability.
Replace gRPC with HTTP/1.1 through an ALB, letting the ALB handle AZ-aware routing.
questions.explanation:
Both A and B are valid patterns, but B is more operationally lightweight AND is Kubernetes-native. Topology Aware Hints (GA in Kubernetes 1.23+) instructs kube-proxy to route to same-zone endpoints when available. Combined with PodTopologySpreadConstraints (ensuring each AZ has pods), you get zone-affinity routing with AZ failover built into the standard Kubernetes primitives — no service mesh complexity.

App Mesh (A) works but adds a service mesh layer you don't need for this. AWS also announced App Mesh deprecation in 2024 — new architecture decisions should avoid it. Collapsing to single AZ (C) violates HA. ALB HTTP/1.1 (D) loses gRPC benefits.
questions.pattern: EKS zone-aware routing: Topology Aware Hints + PodTopologySpreadConstraints is the current Kubernetes-native pattern. Service mesh (App Mesh, Istio) is a valid alternative but heavier. Critical: App Mesh deprecation means new designs should NOT choose it. If you see App Mesh in an option, check the scenario date — for new builds, it's usually a distractor now.
questions.note: AWS announced App Mesh deprecation with end-of-support in September 2026. The exam may still include it as a valid answer for existing scenarios but increasingly prefers native alternatives.

questions.scenario: A company ingests 8 TB/day of telemetry data into an on-premises Hadoop cluster for analytics. They want to migrate to AWS. Current workload: nightly batch ETL (500 jobs over 6 hours), ad-hoc SQL queries from analysts (~200/day), and streaming aggregations over 5-minute windows. The analytics team is heavily SQL-focused. Data must be retained 5 years, with recent 90 days needing interactive query performance. Monthly data growth is 20%.
questions.question: Which target architecture BEST fits the workload mix and team skills?
choices: EMR on EC2 for batch ETL, Amazon Athena for ad-hoc SQL on S3, Kinesis Data Analytics for streaming. Raw and processed data in S3 with Lifecycle to Glacier after 90 days. Glue Data Catalog shared across services.

EMR Serverless for batch ETL (pay-per-job, no cluster management), Amazon Redshift Serverless for interactive SQL over the recent 90 days (with Redshift Spectrum for older S3 data), Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink for streaming aggregations, S3 + Lifecycle for retention, Glue Data Catalog shared.
Migrate the Hadoop cluster to EMR on EC2 exactly as-is, maintaining Hadoop semantics.
AWS Glue for all ETL (batch and streaming), Amazon Athena for all SQL, S3 for storage. No Redshift or EMR.
questions.explanation:
Both A and D are plausible with S3-backed patterns, but neither optimizes for the workload mix. Key insight: '200 ad-hoc queries/day' on 'recent 90 days' with SQL-focused team signals Redshift-class interactive performance, not Athena (which works but is slower and costs more per query at high volume). EMR Serverless replaces EMR on EC2 for bursty ETL without cluster management. Flink handles streaming natively.

Option A uses Athena for 200 queries/day — workable but expensive and slower than Redshift at this volume. Option C is a lift-and-shift that misses serverless benefits. Option D's all-Athena approach doesn't handle 'interactive query performance' well at scale.
questions.pattern: Analytics architecture decomposition: match each workload to the right service. SQL analysts doing frequent interactive queries over hot data → Redshift (or Redshift Serverless). Ad-hoc infrequent queries on cold data → Athena. Batch ETL → EMR Serverless or Glue. Streaming → Flink or Kinesis Data Analytics. Don't jam everything into one service.

questions.scenario: A retail company's order-processing pipeline uses Step Functions to orchestrate 12 steps including Lambda, ECS tasks, and external API calls. State transitions are currently costing $18,000/month. Analysis shows 60% of state transitions are in a high-volume sub-workflow that processes each line item in an order (orders average 8 items, peak 200/second = 1,600 line-item transitions/second). The sub-workflow involves 5 state transitions per item: validate, reserve inventory, calculate tax, apply discount, finalize.
questions.question: Which change MOST effectively reduces Step Functions cost while preserving the workflow?
choices: Switch the parent workflow to Step Functions Express and keep the sub-workflow as Standard.

Switch the line-item sub-workflow (the 60% volume component) to Step Functions Express Workflows, which are priced per-request with sub-second execution and much cheaper for high-volume workloads. Keep the parent workflow as Standard for its longer duration and visibility needs.
Replace Step Functions entirely with EventBridge + Lambda, using EventBridge rules to chain steps.
Consolidate the 5 line-item states into a single Lambda function that performs all 5 operations sequentially, reducing transitions to 1 per item.
questions.explanation:
The trick here is direction: parent is the orchestrator (long-running, visible), sub-workflow is high-volume short-lived. Express Workflows are priced by request count and duration, designed for high-volume short-lived workflows (≤5 min). Standard Workflows are priced per state transition, designed for long-running with full audit trail. Moving the high-volume sub-workflow to Express is the correct optimization.

Option A has it backwards. Option C loses Step Functions' error handling and retry logic. Option D works but makes the Lambda a monolith and loses the per-step visibility that probably matters for a regulated order pipeline.
questions.pattern: Step Functions Standard vs Express: Standard = long-running, per-state-transition pricing, full audit (CloudTrail, X-Ray). Express = short-lived (≤5min), per-request pricing, at-most-once or at-least-once semantics. High-volume short workflows → Express. Nested workflows can mix — Standard parent calling Express children is a common pattern.

references.title: Step Functions Standard vs Express Workflows

questions.scenario: A company has 200 AWS accounts. The security team wants to detect any public S3 buckets across the organization within 5 minutes of creation and automatically remediate by blocking public access. Their current approach: AWS Config conformance pack with s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited and automatic remediation via SSM. Three months in, they observe: detection takes 15-45 minutes, remediation sometimes fails when the bucket has a policy requiring public access for legitimate reasons, and the audit team wants a central dashboard showing ALL security findings, not just S3.
questions.question: Which architectural change BEST addresses all three concerns?
choices: Replace Config with AWS Security Hub enabled across the organization with delegated administration, consume findings from IAM Access Analyzer for actual public exposure (not just config state), route findings through EventBridge to an approval workflow (not auto-remediate), and use Security Hub as the central dashboard.
Keep Config but reduce the evaluation period to 1 minute by increasing Config frequency.
Replace Config with Macie for S3 scanning, since Macie is purpose-built for S3.
Switch to SCPs that deny public bucket ACLs and public bucket policies outright, eliminating the need for detection.
questions.explanation:
Three problems to solve: (1) detection latency, (2) false-positive remediation, (3) unified dashboard. IAM Access Analyzer detects actual public exposure (including via bucket policies, ACLs, or IAM) within minutes, not Config's eventual-consistency schedule. Security Hub aggregates findings from Access Analyzer + GuardDuty + Macie + Config + others, solving the dashboard requirement. Routing through approval (not auto-remediate) handles the false-positive problem.

Option B: Config evaluation frequency isn't tunable to 1 minute — it's effectively event-driven plus periodic. Option C: Macie is for sensitive data discovery, not exposure detection. Option D: SCPs can block public ACLs but some legitimate buckets (static websites, CloudFront origins with OAI needed) may require nuanced public access settings.
questions.pattern: Public exposure detection: IAM Access Analyzer (actual exposure analysis) beats Config rules (configuration state) for speed and accuracy. Unified security findings: Security Hub is the aggregator across GuardDuty, Access Analyzer, Macie, Inspector, Config. Auto-remediation has a false-positive problem; approval workflows are often better.

questions.scenario: A SaaS company runs a multi-tenant application where each tenant has data isolation requirements. They currently use a pool model (shared RDS database, tenant_id column on every table). A new Enterprise tier launches next quarter requiring: dedicated database per tenant for isolation, encryption with tenant-specific KMS keys, ability to backup/restore individual tenants independently, and query performance isolation (one tenant's heavy query must not slow others). Enterprise tier will have 40-60 tenants initially, growing to ~200 over 2 years. Migration cost and ongoing cost matter.
questions.question: Which database architecture BEST meets the Enterprise tier requirements?
choices: Separate RDS/Aurora instance per tenant, each with tenant-specific KMS key, automated snapshots per instance. Use Aurora Serverless v2 to minimize idle cost for low-traffic tenants.
Single Aurora cluster with separate databases (schemas) per tenant and schema-level permissions. Tenant-specific KMS keys for encrypted snapshots.
DynamoDB with a table per tenant, using table-level encryption with tenant-specific KMS keys and on-demand capacity for performance isolation.
Amazon Aurora with a custom endpoint per tenant routed to different replica instances for performance isolation.
questions.explanation:
Silo pattern (instance per tenant) is the exam's expected answer when isolation requirements include: dedicated encryption keys per tenant, independent backup/restore, and query performance isolation. Aurora Serverless v2 is specifically mentioned because it addresses the cost concern — scales to near-zero for idle tenants without the cost of always-on instances. 60 → 200 tenants is manageable with Serverless v2.

Option B (schema-per-tenant in shared cluster) fails 'query performance isolation' — noisy-neighbor tenants still affect others. Option C (DynamoDB table-per-tenant) is a massive refactor from relational, and the scenario doesn't suggest the data model fits DynamoDB. Option D (custom endpoint per tenant) is not a real isolation pattern — replicas share storage.
questions.pattern: SaaS tenant isolation patterns: pool (shared DB, tenant_id column — cheapest, least isolated), bridge (shared DB, schema per tenant — medium), silo (DB per tenant — most isolated, most expensive). Enterprise tier requirements (dedicated keys, independent backups, performance isolation) → silo. Aurora Serverless v2 makes silo economically viable for low-traffic tenants.

questions.scenario: A company runs a REST API on Lambda behind API Gateway. Current traffic: 500M requests/month, average 200ms duration, 256MB memory. Monthly Lambda cost is $8,200. Team wants to reduce cost. Investigation shows: 70% of requests return cached-style responses (same inputs → same outputs for 5-minute windows), the rest execute full logic. Lambda cold starts contribute significant tail latency. Memory profiling shows 180MB peak usage at 512MB allocation.
questions.question: Which optimization combination produces the LARGEST cost reduction without functional regression?
choices: Enable API Gateway caching for the 70% cacheable responses (eliminates Lambda invocations), right-size Lambda memory from 512MB to 256MB based on profiling (50% per-invocation savings), and use Lambda provisioned concurrency for the remaining traffic to eliminate cold starts.

Enable API Gateway caching for cacheable endpoints, right-size Lambda memory to 256MB, and do NOT use provisioned concurrency (since cold starts are a tail latency issue, not a majority cost issue, and provisioned concurrency adds fixed cost that can exceed on-demand for variable traffic).
Migrate Lambda to Fargate for predictable costs.
Switch Lambda runtime to ARM64 (Graviton2) Lambda, which is 20% cheaper.
questions.explanation:
The gotcha: provisioned concurrency sounds attractive but at 500M requests/month it's more expensive than on-demand unless you have sustained traffic shape that justifies it. Cold starts are a latency issue (hitting some requests), not a majority-cost issue. Option A recommends provisioned concurrency as if it were universally good.

Option B is the nuanced correct answer: cache at the front (eliminates 70% of invocations), right-size memory (direct cost reduction on remaining 30%), and explicitly skip provisioned concurrency because the math doesn't support it for this traffic pattern. Option C is a bigger change than needed. Option D gets 20% savings but misses the 70% reduction possible via caching.
questions.pattern: Lambda optimization stack: (1) eliminate invocations via caching — biggest lever, (2) right-size memory based on actual profiling, (3) use Graviton2 for 20% savings if runtime supports it, (4) provisioned concurrency ONLY if you have predictable sustained traffic and cold starts are the primary problem. Provisioned concurrency is a common exam trap — it's not always cheaper.

questions.scenario: A company migrated 300 EC2 workloads from on-premises to AWS using MGN 6 months ago. Post-migration review shows: EC2 instances are mostly older-generation types (m4, c4, r4) matching the source servers, average CPU utilization is 8-12% across the fleet, monthly EC2 spend is $240,000. The team wants to optimize. Constraint: any modernization must not cause application downtime exceeding 15 minutes per workload, and the team has 3 engineers available over 4 months.
questions.question: Which optimization sequence MAXIMIZES savings within the operational constraints?
choices: In parallel: refactor all 300 workloads to containers on ECS Fargate, deploy via blue-green to avoid downtime.

Sequence: (1) use Compute Optimizer to generate rightsizing and modernization recommendations, (2) apply rightsizing to matching workloads via stop/resize/start (within 15-min downtime budget), (3) modernize instance families from m4→m7i, c4→c7i, r4→r7i (price/performance gains), (4) once utilization patterns stabilize, commit to Compute Savings Plans for the new baseline. Refactor to containers only where business value justifies.
Buy 3-year all-upfront EC2 Reserved Instances on current workloads to lock in discount.
Migrate all workloads to Spot instances for maximum savings.
questions.explanation:
Optimization sequence matters: commit-first (C) locks you to inefficient state. Refactor-all (A) is infeasible with 3 engineers over 4 months and downtime budget. Spot-all (D) is reckless for production.

Option B follows the canonical sequence: analyze (Compute Optimizer) → rightsize → modernize instance families (m4→m7i typically gives 15-20% price/performance improvement) → commit to SP at the optimized baseline. Refactor (A's approach) is selective, not blanket. This matches operational reality: rightsizing and modernization can be done with brief stop/resize/start windows, fitting the 15-min downtime budget.
questions.pattern: Post-migration optimization: same rules as cost optimization in general — eliminate waste, rightsize, commit, in that order. Instance family modernization (m4→m7i etc.) is a free lunch — newer generations are cheaper per unit performance. The exam tests whether you pick the staged approach over blanket commitments or blanket refactors.

questions.scenario: A global SaaS application uses Route 53 for DNS. Requirements: (1) route users to the nearest healthy regional endpoint (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1), (2) if a region is unhealthy, traffic must fail over to a designated secondary region (NOT the next-nearest), (3) within a region, distribute traffic across two specific ALBs with different weights (80/20 for canary testing), (4) VIP enterprise customers must be pinned to us-east-1 regardless of their location. DNS changes must propagate globally within 60 seconds.
questions.question: Which Route 53 configuration MEETS all requirements?
choices: Latency-based routing for regional selection, with health checks for failover. Weighted records for within-region traffic split. Geolocation routing for VIP customers.

A multi-layered routing policy: top level geoproximity with bias for VIP IP ranges to us-east-1, latency-based for all other traffic. Within each region, failover routing policy with primary (active) and secondary (designated DR region, not nearest). Within the primary, weighted records 80/20 across ALBs. Health checks on all endpoints. TTL 60 seconds.
Simple routing with multiple values, relying on health checks for failover.
A single weighted policy splitting traffic evenly across all regional ALBs, with health checks removing unhealthy endpoints.
questions.explanation:
Real Route 53 designs often require multiple policies in a tree. Requirements map to: (1) latency-based for nearest → but VIP override needs a conditional upstream, so geoproximity with custom bias or IP-based steering; (2) designated secondary region isn't 'next nearest' — you need explicit failover policy with primary/secondary, not latency fallback; (3) 80/20 within region → weighted; (4) VIP pinning → overrides need to happen before latency selection.

Option A misses the 'designated secondary region' requirement — latency-based fallback goes to next-nearest, not a designated region. Options C and D miss most requirements. Option B layers the policies correctly: geoproximity (or IP-based) for VIP override → latency-based otherwise → failover with explicit secondary → weighted within region.
questions.pattern: Complex Route 53 designs layer routing policies: one policy's record targets another policy. Geolocation/geoproximity for regulatory/VIP overrides, latency for performance, failover for DR, weighted for canary, multivalue for multiple IPs with health checks. Memorize which can stack on which. 'Designated DR region' ≠ 'next-nearest healthy region' — that's a failover policy, not latency fallback.

questions.scenario: A hybrid enterprise uses AWS IAM Identity Center with Microsoft Active Directory as the identity source (synced via AD Connector). Security requires: (1) workforce users access AWS only from corporate devices, (2) AWS admin access requires hardware MFA (YubiKey), (3) contractor access expires automatically after 90 days, (4) all access attempts audited in a dedicated central account, with failed attempts triggering alerts.
questions.question: Which combination BEST enforces these controls?
choices: IAM Identity Center permission sets with session policies restricting by IP ranges (corporate network). MFA policy requiring any MFA (hardware OR software). Manual contractor offboarding via HR process.

IAM Identity Center with: (1) session policies or SCPs using aws:SourceIp and aws:ViaAWSService conditions for corporate device attestation (combined with VPN or AWS Client VPN for off-corporate-network access), (2) MFA policy in IAM Identity Center requiring FIDO-compliant authenticators (hardware-backed), (3) automated contractor deprovisioning via Lambda triggered by AD group expiry or Identity Center API, (4) CloudTrail organization trail with Athena queries and Security Hub alerting for failed sign-ins.
Replace Identity Center with IAM users created directly in each account, enforcing MFA and IP restrictions via IAM policies.
Use AWS Directory Service Managed AD with custom trust relationships and rely on AD's native audit logs.
questions.explanation:
Each requirement maps to a specific control: corporate device enforcement (IP restrictions as a proxy; true device attestation requires Verified Access or similar), hardware MFA (FIDO/WebAuthn requirement in Identity Center MFA policy), automated contractor expiry (Identity Center doesn't natively auto-expire — needs Lambda automation tied to AD group membership or explicit schedule), audit (CloudTrail organization trail + Security Hub for alerting).

Option A uses 'any MFA' which violates hardware-only requirement, and manual offboarding misses contractor auto-expiry. Option C abandons Identity Center for a much worse per-account model. Option D doesn't directly address the requirements.
questions.pattern: Identity Center security controls: MFA type restriction (require FIDO/WebAuthn for hardware keys), session policies for IP/network restrictions, no native temporary access expiry (needs automation), CloudTrail for audit. Identity Center is the strategic direction but lacks some enterprise features out-of-box — the exam tests whether you know what requires custom Lambda/EventBridge automation.

references.title: Temporary elevated access with Identity Center

questions.scenario: A media streaming service uses ElastiCache Redis for session storage (~15 GB active dataset, 500K operations/sec peak, 2ms p99 latency SLA). During a recent AZ outage, the cache in that AZ became unavailable for 12 minutes, causing session loss for affected users. The team wants to eliminate this outage class while respecting: existing clients use a single Redis endpoint (minimize client-side changes), data durability within 5 seconds of a write, and regional HA.
questions.question: Which ElastiCache configuration BEST addresses these requirements?
choices: ElastiCache Redis in cluster mode enabled, with replica per primary across 3 AZs, Multi-AZ with automatic failover enabled, and data-tiering to reduce costs.

ElastiCache Redis (cluster mode disabled) in a replication group with primary + 2 read replicas across 3 AZs, Multi-AZ auto-failover enabled. Clients continue using the single primary endpoint which Multi-AZ remaps on failover. Accept the standard replication (async) given 5-second durability window is achievable.
Switch to ElastiCache for Memcached with auto-discovery for the multi-node simplicity.
Deploy self-managed Redis Sentinel on EC2 across 3 AZs for custom failover control.
questions.explanation:
The single-endpoint requirement is the subtle gate. Cluster mode enabled (A) shards data across shards — clients need cluster-aware Redis libraries and the endpoint model changes. Cluster mode disabled (B) keeps a single primary endpoint; Multi-AZ failover seamlessly remaps which node is primary. This matches 'single Redis endpoint' and 'minimize client-side changes.'

Memcached (C) doesn't have replication or durability — wrong tool for session storage needing 5-second durability. Self-managed Sentinel (D) adds operational burden ElastiCache eliminates. Option A is tempting (cluster mode sounds more HA) but changes the client model.
questions.pattern: ElastiCache Redis flavors: cluster mode disabled = single primary + replicas, standard Redis client, one endpoint, vertical scaling only. Cluster mode enabled = sharded, cluster-aware client required, horizontal scaling. 'Single endpoint' or 'existing client compatibility' → cluster mode disabled. Large datasets beyond one node's capacity → cluster mode enabled.

references.title: ElastiCache Redis cluster mode enabled vs disabled

questions.scenario: A company runs a data processing workload on EKS with 200 worker pods processing messages from SQS. Each message triggers 5-15 seconds of CPU-bound work. Current setup: 50 m5.xlarge EC2 nodes in the EKS cluster, running 200 pods, ~70% average CPU utilization, monthly EC2 spend $24,000. Queue depth varies — sometimes empty for an hour, sometimes 100,000 messages backlog. The team wants to reduce cost without impacting throughput during backlogs.
questions.question: Which change produces the BEST cost/scalability outcome?
choices: Replace EC2 node groups with Fargate for all pods; Fargate per-pod pricing eliminates over-provisioning and idle waste.

Use Karpenter for EKS node provisioning with consolidation enabled and a mix of Spot instances (capacity-optimized, diverse instance types) for the bulk of worker pods, keeping a small on-demand baseline. Combine with Horizontal Pod Autoscaler based on SQS queue depth (via KEDA or custom metrics). Consider EKS node group scaling only if Karpenter is not adopted.
Switch to Lambda with SQS trigger, eliminating EKS entirely.
Use Cluster Autoscaler with Spot-only node groups.
questions.explanation:
Options A, B, C, and D are all plausible — the trap is picking without considering the workload characteristics. Fargate (A) eliminates over-provisioning but costs ~20% more per vCPU/GB than EC2; for sustained throughput workloads, EC2 Spot is typically cheaper. Lambda (C) works for 5-15 second tasks but has 15-min max and cold-start considerations; if you can migrate, it can be cheapest but it's a refactor.

Option B is the best nuanced answer: Karpenter is the modern EKS node provisioner (replacing Cluster Autoscaler) that can mix Spot and on-demand intelligently, consolidate underused nodes, and provision the right instance types just-in-time. HPA based on SQS queue depth (via KEDA) scales pods to match backlog. This achieves cost reduction without throughput loss during bursts. Option D (Cluster Autoscaler + Spot-only) is risky — production workloads usually want some on-demand baseline.
questions.pattern: EKS cost optimization modern stack: Karpenter (vs Cluster Autoscaler) + Spot with on-demand baseline + KEDA for event-driven pod scaling. Fargate per-pod has simplicity benefits but higher unit cost — use for inconsistent/low-volume workloads where you're paying for idle EC2. Lambda for bursty short-duration tasks if you can refactor.

questions.scenario: A global retail API serves 10,000 req/sec peak. Existing architecture: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Read heavy (95% reads), with repeating query patterns. Current p99 latency: 180ms, driven mostly by DynamoDB query latency. Budget for optimization is limited. Team wants to reduce p99 latency significantly AND reduce DynamoDB cost.
questions.question: Which change BEST achieves both goals?
choices: Add DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of DynamoDB, configured inside the same VPC as Lambda. DAX provides microsecond reads for cached items and reduces DynamoDB read capacity consumption for repeated queries.
Migrate DynamoDB to Amazon Aurora for better query performance.
Add ElastiCache Redis in front of DynamoDB, implement custom caching logic in Lambda.
Add API Gateway caching at the edge for all GET endpoints.
questions.explanation:
Both A and C add caching, and both work. The difference: DAX is a DynamoDB-native cache (same API as DynamoDB, no application code changes, write-through cache for reads), while ElastiCache requires custom cache logic in the application. DAX is the lower-effort, lower-risk answer when the read store is already DynamoDB and the access patterns are DynamoDB-native queries.

Option B is a massive migration, not an optimization. Option C works but requires custom caching code. Option D (API Gateway caching) works for idempotent GETs but doesn't help DynamoDB cost if Lambda invocations still happen — and caching user-specific responses at API Gateway can have security implications.
questions.pattern: DynamoDB + DAX vs DynamoDB + ElastiCache: DAX is DynamoDB-specific (same API, microsecond latency for cached items, automatic write-through). ElastiCache is generic (requires app-level caching logic but more flexibility). For DynamoDB read-heavy workloads with repeating patterns and limited budget, DAX is usually the better-fit first answer.

questions.scenario: A financial company runs 50 on-premises VMware workloads containing regulated data (GDPR, PCI-DSS). Requirements for migration: (1) minimal application refactoring, (2) maintain existing IP addresses to avoid firewall rule changes in connected on-premises systems, (3) preserve VMware operational familiarity for the ops team during a 2-year transition period, (4) phase workloads to AWS-native services opportunistically after initial migration. Network: 10 Gbps Direct Connect.
questions.question: Which target platform BEST satisfies these requirements?
choices: AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) to rehost as EC2 with new IPs; update on-premises firewall rules to match.

VMware Cloud on AWS (VMC) for initial migration — preserves VMware operational model and IP address ranges (via stretched L2 networks), then use AWS services alongside VMC for gradual modernization. After stabilization, selectively migrate workloads from VMC to native EC2/ECS/Lambda as modernization matures.
CloudEndure Migration (the previous tool, now MGN) for initial rehost, then lift-and-shift everything immediately to AWS-native.
Deploy the workloads on EC2 Dedicated Hosts to replicate the VMware isolation model.
questions.explanation:
The requirement stack points specifically to VMware Cloud on AWS: (1) minimal refactoring ✓ (VMs move as-is), (2) IP preservation ✓ (VMC supports stretched L2 via HCX), (3) VMware familiarity ✓ (vSphere UI, vMotion), (4) gradual modernization ✓ (VMC integrates with AWS services). This is the exact scenario VMC is designed for.

Option A breaks IP preservation. Option C is obsolete (CloudEndure is MGN). Option D (Dedicated Hosts) doesn't replicate VMware — it's just dedicated EC2 hardware. VMC is expensive and wouldn't be a default, but specific requirements (IP preservation, VMware familiarity, stretched L2) make it the right answer here.
questions.pattern: VMware Cloud on AWS is a niche tool with specific use cases: IP address preservation (stretched L2), VMware operational continuity during transition, hardware dependencies (USB dongles etc.), or speed-to-cloud when refactoring isn't possible. It's expensive and NOT a default migration target, but the exam tests whether you recognize when it IS the right answer based on specific constraints.

references.title: When to choose VMC vs MGN

questions.scenario: A company runs a high-security enclave in a dedicated VPC that must NOT have internet access at all. Workloads in the enclave need to: pull container images from ECR, access S3 objects, invoke Lambda functions, read Secrets Manager secrets, make API calls to DynamoDB, and receive EventBridge events from other accounts. The security team wants the minimum number of VPC endpoints while supporting all these requirements. They also want to detect any configuration that would accidentally allow internet egress.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST meets these requirements?
choices: Deploy a single NAT Gateway with a restrictive route table to limit internet access.

Create VPC interface endpoints for ECR (ecr.api, ecr.dkr), Secrets Manager, Lambda, EventBridge, and STS; create gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB. Remove all internet gateways, NAT gateways, and VPC peering to non-private networks. Use AWS Network Firewall logging or VPC Flow Logs with IMDSv2/drops to detect any unintended egress paths. Enable IAM Access Analyzer external access findings.
Create interface endpoints for all services including S3 and DynamoDB (gateway endpoints are legacy).
Use AWS PrivateLink service endpoints for each service via a centralized services VPC, accessed via Transit Gateway.
questions.explanation:
The distinction to know: S3 and DynamoDB have GATEWAY endpoints (free, route-table-based), everything else uses INTERFACE endpoints (PrivateLink, hourly cost + data processing). ECR requires TWO endpoints (ecr.api for API calls, ecr.dkr for container pulls). STS is easy to miss — many workloads need it for role assumption. EventBridge cross-account events reach this VPC via the interface endpoint.

Option A still allows internet via NAT — violates requirement. Option C has gateway endpoints as 'legacy' which is false — they're the recommended, free option for S3 and DynamoDB. Option D is more complex than needed; a centralized endpoints VPC with TGW is a valid enterprise pattern but not required for a single enclave VPC.
questions.pattern: VPC endpoint types: Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB only) — free, route table entries. Interface endpoints (everything else) — hourly + data charges, PrivateLink under the hood. ECR needs both ecr.api AND ecr.dkr. STS is commonly forgotten. A true no-internet VPC uses endpoints for AWS services + Flow Logs / Network Firewall to audit any accidental egress paths.

questions.scenario: A company has accumulated 8 PB of data in S3 Standard over 5 years. Analysis: 1.5 PB is accessed weekly, 2 PB is accessed monthly, 2 PB is accessed quarterly, 2 PB has not been accessed in 18+ months. Management demands 40% storage cost reduction without losing any data and without impacting production access latency for frequently-used objects. Data has mixed sizes (some MB-scale, many KB-scale small files).
questions.question: Which approach achieves the cost target while respecting the constraints?
choices: Move all 8 PB to Intelligent-Tiering and let it manage automatically.

Move all data accessed within 90 days to Intelligent-Tiering with Archive Access and Deep Archive Access tiers enabled. For the 2 PB 'quarterly accessed' subset, evaluate whether the access pattern is predictable enough for Lifecycle to Glacier Flexible Retrieval (millisecond vs minutes retrieval trade-off). For the 2 PB unaccessed 18+ months, Lifecycle to Glacier Deep Archive. Important: check object sizes — S3 Intelligent-Tiering has a minimum billable size of 128 KB, and Glacier has higher minimums; for very small files, consider S3 Standard-IA or keeping in Standard.
Move all data to Glacier Deep Archive regardless of access pattern.
Use only Lifecycle policies transitioning objects through Standard → Standard-IA → Glacier Flexible Retrieval → Deep Archive based on age.
questions.explanation:
The gotcha: Intelligent-Tiering (and Glacier classes) have minimum billable object sizes (128KB for Intelligent-Tiering). Moving small files to Intelligent-Tiering can INCREASE cost because each file is billed as 128KB minimum. The 'many KB-scale small files' in the scenario is a deliberate hint.

Option A ignores the small-file issue. Option C violates 'don't impact production latency' — quarterly-accessed data can't wait hours for Deep Archive retrieval. Option D is purely age-based Lifecycle which doesn't adapt to varying access patterns for the weekly/monthly tier. Option B is the nuanced answer that addresses each subset with appropriate storage class and flags the small-file minimum billable size trap.
questions.pattern: Storage class gotchas: Intelligent-Tiering has 128KB minimum billable object size (small files get more expensive). Glacier classes have retrieval fees. Standard-IA has minimum storage duration (30 days). Lifecycle transitions have per-object fees. For mixed object sizes, storage class choice may differ by size, not just age. The exam tests whether you read 'many small files' as the trap it is.

references.title: S3 pricing and minimum object sizes

questions.scenario: A healthcare company stores PHI in Aurora PostgreSQL. Compliance requires: encryption at rest with keys managed by the company (not AWS), automatic key rotation annually, cryptographic separation of different customer tenants' data, and the ability to revoke access to a specific tenant's data by destroying their key. The solution must minimize operational key-management overhead while meeting these requirements.
questions.question: Which key management approach MEETS these requirements?
choices: AWS-managed KMS keys (aws/rds) with automatic rotation — operationally simplest.

Customer-managed KMS keys in KMS: one master key per tenant, automatic annual rotation enabled on each key, Aurora cluster encrypted with a top-level CMK but tenant data encrypted client-side (application-layer) with per-tenant keys via KMS Encrypt/Decrypt APIs or envelope encryption.
AWS CloudHSM with customer-managed keys in a dedicated HSM cluster; application encrypts/decrypts data using CloudHSM SDK.
Customer-managed KMS key for the Aurora cluster encryption, combined with Aurora PostgreSQL's pgcrypto extension for application-level tenant-specific encryption.
questions.explanation:
Both B and D are plausible. The key distinction: Aurora's cluster-level encryption uses ONE key for the whole cluster — you can't have per-tenant cluster encryption with different keys. So tenant-level cryptographic separation requires application-layer encryption with per-tenant keys, not just cluster-level encryption.

Option B handles this correctly: cluster-level encryption with a master CMK (protects storage) + application-layer per-tenant encryption with individual CMKs (protects tenant separation and enables key-destruction revocation). Option D uses pgcrypto which moves encryption into the DB but still needs per-tenant key management logic. CloudHSM (C) meets crypto requirements but is operationally heavy — scenario asks for minimum operational overhead. Option A's AWS-managed keys can't be deleted/revoked or directly controlled.
questions.pattern: Cryptographic tenant separation in shared databases: cluster encryption uses ONE key for the whole cluster. Per-tenant key separation requires application-layer encryption. Destroying a key (key material deletion) is the hard-revocation model — it only works when the data is encrypted with a key that's specifically tenant-scoped. CloudHSM vs KMS: CloudHSM for single-tenant HSM requirements (FIPS 140-2 L3), KMS for everything else.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating 80 Oracle databases from on-premises to AWS. Profile: 30 databases are <500 GB used for OLTP, 40 are 500 GB - 5 TB mixed OLTP/reporting, 10 are 5-20 TB data warehouse workloads. Oracle licensing is a key cost driver. The company wants to escape Oracle licensing on 70% of workloads within 18 months while maintaining critical performance. Developers have varying levels of SQL knowledge but no Oracle-specific schema conversion experience.
questions.question: Which migration strategy BEST fits this portfolio?
choices: Migrate all 80 databases to Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL using SCT for schema conversion and DMS for data migration.

Portfolio-specific approach: (1) Small/medium OLTP (70 databases) → SCT assessment + Aurora PostgreSQL (or Babelfish for PostgreSQL-compatible SQL Server-like compatibility where Oracle usage is light). (2) Data warehouse workloads (10 databases) → Amazon Redshift after schema conversion, accepting the refactor cost because the performance characteristics justify it. (3) Very complex OLTP workloads with heavy PL/SQL that SCT flags as 'high effort' → keep on RDS for Oracle temporarily, revisit in a later phase. Use DMS for all data migration.
Lift-and-shift all 80 to RDS for Oracle to avoid refactoring, accept Oracle licensing cost.
Migrate all to DynamoDB for lowest licensing cost and serverless operation.
questions.explanation:
Portfolio approaches win on mixed migration scenarios. Key insights: (1) target database differs by workload type — Aurora PostgreSQL for OLTP, Redshift for DW, (2) SCT assessment gives per-database complexity scoring — some may be keep-on-Oracle initially, (3) 70% target doesn't require 100% migration — acknowledge some databases stay on Oracle temporarily.

Option A's blanket Aurora PostgreSQL migration ignores that DW workloads are much better on Redshift. Option C doesn't escape licensing. Option D is absurd for relational workloads. The exam rewards portfolio thinking over blanket strategies.
questions.pattern: Oracle exit portfolio: different targets for different workload profiles. OLTP → Aurora PostgreSQL. DW → Redshift. Very complex PL/SQL → RDS for Oracle as interim. SCT assessment is the first step, not optional. 70% migration target is realistic; 100% is often not economically sensible. Babelfish is for SQL Server-to-Postgres compatibility, not Oracle.

questions.scenario:
A company operates 80 AWS accounts in an Organization. The security team uses a central Security Tooling account as delegated administrator for GuardDuty, Security Hub, and IAM Access Analyzer. They want to also delegate administration of AWS Config aggregation to a DIFFERENT account (the Audit account) while keeping the other services delegated to Security Tooling. The teams are organizationally separate and must not have access to each other's consoles.

A junior engineer claims 'you can only have one delegated admin per Organization.'
questions.question: Which statement about delegated administration is correct, and what should the company do?
choices: The junior engineer is correct; consolidate all security tooling under one delegated admin account.

Delegated administration is configured per-service; the company can have Security Tooling as delegated admin for GuardDuty/Security Hub/Access Analyzer, and Audit as delegated admin for Config. Use RegisterDelegatedAdministrator separately for each service.
Use two AWS Organizations; delegate each service in its respective Organization.
Delegated admin only works for the management account; run Config directly from the management account.
questions.explanation:
Delegated administration is configured PER SERVICE, not per Organization. Each service that supports delegation maintains its own delegated-admin registration. You can and often should split delegation across accounts — e.g., GuardDuty/Security Hub to a security operations account, Config aggregation to an audit account, CloudFormation StackSets to a deployment account.

Option A is a common misconception. Option C is absurd (can't have two Orgs). Option D contradicts the delegation feature.

The subtle exam trap: 'delegated admin' sounds singular but isn't. Services that support it: GuardDuty, Security Hub, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, Config, CloudFormation StackSets, Firewall Manager, Audit Manager, Detective, and more — each independently.
questions.pattern: Delegated administration is per-service. Multiple services can have DIFFERENT delegated admin accounts. When the scenario has organizationally separate security/audit/operations teams, splitting delegation is often the right answer — even though most examples show one 'security tooling' account.

references.title: AWS Organizations — Services that work with delegated admin
AWS Config aggregator with delegated admin

questions.scenario: A multi-region application uses Aurora Global Database (primary in us-east-1, secondary in eu-west-1). The team needs to run read-heavy analytics queries in eu-west-1 against current data — staleness up to 1 second is acceptable. Separately, a regulatory requirement mandates that EU customer write traffic be processed in EU regions. They're considering Aurora Global Database write forwarding, but the application stack uses database-level stored procedures and triggers extensively.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST satisfies both the analytics and regulatory requirements?
choices: Enable Aurora Global Database write forwarding in eu-west-1 so EU writes appear local but are forwarded to us-east-1. Analytics queries run against the eu-west-1 replica.
Use Aurora Global Database in managed failover with automatic region promotion; manually promote eu-west-1 to primary when EU traffic spikes.

Use Aurora Global Database for analytics in eu-west-1 (reads only), but route EU writes via application-layer logic to a SEPARATE Aurora cluster physically located in eu-west-1 that replicates asynchronously to us-east-1 via DMS. Accept the architectural complexity.
Use Aurora Global Database in standard configuration; route all writes to us-east-1 regardless of customer region. Document the regulatory non-compliance for later remediation.
questions.explanation:
This is a hard tradeoff question. Write forwarding (A) SOUNDS right but has a critical limitation: forwarded writes are committed in us-east-1, meaning EU customer data transits to and is processed in the US region — this likely violates the data-residency requirement depending on interpretation. Write forwarding also has limitations with DDL, stored procedures, and triggers.

Manual promotion (B) doesn't solve steady-state — you'd constantly failover. Option D knowingly violates compliance. Option C is architecturally uglier but actually satisfies the regulatory requirement: EU writes are processed and stored in EU infrastructure.

The subtle trap: Aurora Global Database write forwarding is marketed as 'multi-region writes' but the commit happens in the primary region. If 'data cannot leave region X' is strict, write forwarding violates it.
questions.pattern: Aurora Global Database write forwarding: writes APPEAR local to the application but COMMIT in the primary region. For latency-tolerant apps that need simplified multi-region write logic, it's great. For strict data-residency, it's insufficient — writes still transit to the primary region. Read the compliance requirement carefully.
questions.note: Aurora Global Database write forwarding also has restrictions on DDL, transactions, and certain query types — check current docs before assuming it works for any workload.

questions.scenario: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) federated with their external IdP (Okta) for workforce access to 100 AWS accounts. A new requirement: developers must be able to assume roles in accounts, but only during business hours (9am–6pm Eastern, Mon–Fri). The security team rejected implementing this in the IdP because business-hours enforcement must be on the AWS side for auditability. Break-glass emergency access must still work 24/7.
questions.question: Which approach MOST effectively implements this on the AWS side?
choices: Permission sets with inline IAM policies using aws:CurrentTime condition keys to restrict actions to business hours, with a separate break-glass permission set excluded from this restriction.
SCP at the developer OU using aws:CurrentTime and aws:RequestedRegion to deny actions outside business hours. Break-glass uses a role in the management account excluded via aws:PrincipalArn.
Scheduled Lambda function enabling/disabling the permission sets assigned to developers at 9am and 6pm ET.
CloudTrail event rules that detect out-of-hours actions and automatically revoke session tokens via an EventBridge-triggered Lambda.
questions.explanation:
This is an SCP vs permission-set ambiguity question. Both A and B could technically work, but B is better for two reasons:

(1) SCPs cannot be bypassed by any principal including root, so enforcement is guaranteed at the Org boundary. Permission set policies (A) can be modified by admins or supplemented by principals with additional policies.

(2) Permission-set inline policies (A) would need to be applied to EVERY permission set for EVERY developer across multi-account assignments. SCP at the OU level is one policy, centrally managed.

Break-glass exclusion via aws:PrincipalArn is the standard pattern. Scheduled Lambda (C) has timing precision issues and operational fragility. EventBridge detection (D) is reactive/detective, not preventive.

The subtle trap: aws:CurrentTime uses UTC, so the SCP condition would be against 13:00–22:00 UTC during EST (or 14:00–23:00 UTC during EDT) — daylight saving adds complexity. A common exam distractor is 'use aws:CurrentTime with local times' which fails at DST boundaries.
questions.pattern: Time-based access restrictions → aws:CurrentTime or aws:EpochTime in SCPs (preferred over permission-set policies for multi-account scale). Break-glass → aws:PrincipalArn exclusion. DST gotcha: AWS condition keys are in UTC — translate business hours to UTC considering DST, or use EpochTime ranges.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating 300 VMware VMs to AWS. They have a 10 Gbps Direct Connect. Total data size is 80 TB. Business constraints: migration window is 30 days; applications can tolerate rolling cutover per-app (~2-hour windows each); the team wants continuous replication so that cutover is a fast switch. However, three of the 300 VMs are heavily transactional databases (12 TB, 4 TB, 6 TB) with extremely high write rates — AWS MGN testing shows replication lag exceeding acceptable thresholds for these three.
questions.question: Which approach BEST handles the mixed portfolio?
choices: Use AWS MGN for all 300 VMs including the three high-write databases; increase MGN replication server capacity until lag is acceptable.

Use AWS MGN for the 297 standard VMs. For the three high-write databases, use AWS DMS with CDC for the database-specific migration path, sizing DMS replication instances appropriately and performing the cutover during a low-write window.
Use AWS Snowball Edge for the three large databases and MGN for the rest.
Delay the three databases to a separate project; migrate only the 297 VMs in the 30-day window.
questions.explanation:
This is a 'right tool for the job per workload' question. AWS MGN is excellent for OS-level rehosting of VMs but replicates at the block/filesystem level, which struggles with extremely high database write rates — the replication lag compounds. For transactional databases with high writes, DMS with CDC (transaction-level replication) is purpose-built and handles write rate much more efficiently.

Option A (increase MGN capacity) will hit fundamental limits — block-level replication of constantly-changing data is inherently less efficient than log-based CDC. Option C (Snowball) is wrong because you have 10 Gbps DX — bandwidth isn't the problem, write rate is. Option D violates the 30-day window.

The subtle trap: defaulting to 'MGN for everything' because it's the universal rehost tool. The 7Rs framework includes within-rehost choices — sometimes MGN for OS-level rehost plus DMS for DB-level rehost is the right pattern.
questions.pattern: Migration tool selection is per-workload. MGN = OS-level block replication, great for general VMs, struggles with high-write DBs. DMS = database-level log replication, specific to DB engines. When a scenario calls out extreme write rates or specific database engines, DMS may win over MGN even for rehost-class migrations. Snowball is for bandwidth constraints, not write-rate constraints.

references.title: AWS Database Migration Service with CDC
Choosing between MGN and DMS for migrations

questions.scenario: A company's Compute Savings Plan portfolio covers their EC2 baseline. They're about to migrate 40% of their EC2 workload to ECS Fargate as part of modernization over 6 months. The finance team asks whether the existing Compute SPs will continue to provide value after the migration or whether they should let them expire and purchase new commitments after modernization.
questions.question: Which answer is CORRECT about Compute Savings Plan coverage of Fargate?
choices: Compute Savings Plans DO NOT cover Fargate; they only apply to EC2. The company should let existing SPs run out and purchase EC2 Instance SPs or Fargate-specific commitments after modernization.

Compute Savings Plans cover EC2, Fargate, AND Lambda automatically under the same commitment. Existing Compute SPs continue providing value through the migration — savings shift from EC2 spend to Fargate spend as workloads move. Committed $/hour is the measure, not workload type.
Compute Savings Plans cover Fargate but at a lower discount percentage than EC2 — the company will see reduced savings post-migration and should consider purchasing Fargate-specific SPs.
Compute Savings Plans only cover Fargate if the Fargate tasks run in the same region as the Savings Plan was purchased in.
questions.explanation:
Compute Savings Plans cover EC2, Fargate, AND Lambda — all three under one commitment. The commitment is $/hour; whichever of the three services consumes that commitment at the appropriate discount rate gets the savings. This makes Compute SPs uniquely valuable during modernization because savings automatically flow from retiring EC2 to new Fargate/Lambda spend.

This is in contrast to EC2 Instance Savings Plans (which cover only EC2, locked to an instance family and region) and Reserved Instances (even narrower locks). Compute SPs trade some discount for flexibility — typically ~66% vs ~72% for EC2 Instance SPs on 3-year all-upfront, but the flexibility during modernization is the whole point.

The subtle trap: options that sound plausible ('lower discount for Fargate,' 'region-locked') exploit uncertainty about Compute SP scope. Compute SPs are uniformly discounted across the three compute services.
questions.pattern: Compute SP flexibility: covers EC2 + Fargate + Lambda, cross-region, cross-family, cross-size, cross-OS, cross-tenancy. EC2 Instance SP: locked to family-in-region. RI: narrowest. During modernization, Compute SPs are the only commitment type that survives the transition. The exam tests knowledge of this specific scope.

references.title: AWS Savings Plans types and coverage

questions.scenario: An architecture uses Step Functions to orchestrate a 6-step workflow per customer transaction. Current: Standard Workflows at ~40M executions/month costing approximately $1,000/month. The team is considering Express Workflows for cost reduction. Each workflow takes ~15 seconds end-to-end and doesn't require visual debugging of individual executions for every run. Compliance requires that if an execution fails, the failure details must be queryable for 90 days.
questions.question: Which change MOST effectively reduces Step Functions cost while meeting compliance?
choices: Switch to Express Workflows. Express has no per-execution history, but logging to CloudWatch Logs can preserve failure details for compliance queries. Configure CloudWatch Logs retention to 90 days.
Switch to Express Workflows synchronous mode, which retains execution history like Standard.
Stay on Standard Workflows because Express doesn't meet any compliance need.
Switch to Lambda chaining to eliminate Step Functions costs entirely.
questions.explanation:
Express Workflows cost FAR less than Standard (~$1 per million state transitions vs $25 per million) but lack built-in execution history. The standard Express pattern for compliance: ship execution logs to CloudWatch Logs (or S3 via Firehose) with appropriate retention. Failure details are queryable via CloudWatch Logs Insights.

Option B is wrong — Express Workflows don't 'retain execution history like Standard' even in synchronous mode; they differ fundamentally in state management. Option C is overly conservative. Option D (Lambda chaining) loses orchestration benefits and introduces retry/error-handling complexity.

The subtle trap: 'Express Workflows can't be used for compliance' is a common misconception because of the missing execution history UI. Compliance doesn't require a UI — it requires queryable records. CloudWatch Logs satisfies that.

Cost math: 40M executions × 6 transitions = 240M transitions/month. Standard = $25 × 240 = $6,000/month (not $1,000 — original number may reflect mix). Express = $1 × 240 = $240/month. Adding CloudWatch Logs cost (~$50/month for this volume) still nets large savings.
questions.pattern: Step Functions Standard vs Express: Express is far cheaper (~25× less) but lacks execution history UI, limited to 5-minute workflows, no .waitForTaskToken. For short, high-volume workflows where logs suffice for compliance, Express is usually right. Standard when you need long-running, visual debugging, or task tokens.

references.title: Step Functions — Standard vs Express Workflows

questions.scenario: A company has three AWS accounts that aren't in their main Organization — they were created by an acquired company and are still on separate billing. The main Organization has SCPs restricting certain services in the production OU. Leadership wants the three acquired accounts to be governed by the same SCPs NOW, without waiting for the account-migration process (expected in 6 months).
questions.question: What is the ACCURATE assessment of this request?
choices: Apply the SCPs to the three accounts individually using AWS CLI RegisterAccount calls.
Invite the three accounts to the main Organization as member accounts; they will inherit SCPs immediately while billing can remain separate through consolidated billing tags.

SCPs can only be applied to accounts that are members of the Organization that contains the SCP. The three acquired accounts must be invited to the Organization first (or moved via the account removal+invite process) before SCPs apply. There is no way to apply SCPs cross-Organization.
Use Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share the SCP policies across to the acquired accounts' Organization.
questions.explanation:
SCPs are strictly an AWS Organizations construct — they apply only to accounts that are members of the Organization where the SCP is defined. You cannot apply SCPs to accounts in a different Organization, and you cannot apply SCPs to standalone accounts.

Option A: 'RegisterAccount' isn't a valid API call for this purpose. Option B partially correct direction but wrong on billing — joining an Organization means consolidated billing; you can't maintain 'separate billing' within the same Org in the way described. Option D: RAM shares resources, not policies; SCPs cannot be shared via RAM.

The company has exactly two options to bring the three accounts under SCP governance: (1) invite them to join the main Organization (accepting consolidated billing), or (2) wait for the account migration. There's no third way.

The subtle trap: SCPs feel like 'policies' that might be exportable/shareable, but they're fundamentally tied to Organization membership. The exam tests whether you understand this scope limitation.
questions.pattern: SCPs are Organization-scoped — they only apply to member accounts of the Organization where the SCP exists. No cross-Organization SCP, no standalone-account SCP, no RAM-based SCP sharing. The only way for an account to be governed by an SCP is to join the Organization that contains it.

questions.scenario: A container workload runs on EKS with 30 microservices communicating via service mesh. The team currently uses AWS App Mesh but has been told by their AWS account team that App Mesh is deprecated and they should plan migration. They evaluated alternatives: VPC Lattice, Istio on EKS, and Amazon ECS Service Connect. The workload is EKS-native, needs mTLS between services, supports canary deployments between service versions, and the platform team wants minimal operational burden.
questions.question: Which migration path BEST fits?
choices: VPC Lattice — it's the AWS successor to App Mesh, provides mTLS, supports canary weights, and integrates natively with EKS/ECS/Lambda without the operational burden of self-managed Istio.
Istio on EKS — it's the industry standard service mesh with the richest feature set.
ECS Service Connect — AWS's managed service mesh for containers.
Stay on App Mesh until it's fully retired; new development can use a different mesh.
questions.explanation:
VPC Lattice is AWS's current direction for service-to-service networking — it's an application-layer networking service (not exclusively a 'mesh' but fills that role) supporting mTLS, weighted routing (canary), cross-VPC and cross-account service networking, and integrates with EKS, ECS, EC2, and Lambda. AWS has been consolidating App Mesh users onto VPC Lattice.

Istio (B) is powerful but requires significant operational investment — contradicts 'minimal operational burden.' ECS Service Connect (C) is ECS-specific and doesn't apply to this EKS workload. Staying on App Mesh (D) defers the problem without solving it.

The subtle complication: VPC Lattice isn't a drop-in 'service mesh' in the Istio sense — it operates at a different layer (Lattice uses its own networking plane, not sidecars by default). The mental model 'App Mesh → Lattice' is the AWS-promoted path but involves real architectural changes.
questions.note: AWS has announced App Mesh deprecation (with end-of-support dates). Check current AWS announcements for exact timelines. VPC Lattice is the recommended successor for AWS-native service networking needs, though teams with heavy Istio investment may prefer Istio on EKS.
questions.pattern: App Mesh → VPC Lattice is the AWS-recommended successor path. VPC Lattice is AWS's current answer for 'service-to-service networking.' If the scenario asks 'replacement for App Mesh' with AWS-native preference, Lattice is the expected answer. Istio remains valid for teams wanting portability and feature richness at the cost of operational burden.

references.title: AWS App Mesh to VPC Lattice migration

questions.scenario: A company is migrating 200 databases from on-premises to AWS. 80 are SQL Server, 60 are Oracle, 50 are MySQL/PostgreSQL, 10 are DB2. The team wants to avoid relicensing on AWS where possible. They have Microsoft Enterprise Agreement with License Mobility benefits and Oracle licenses with Oracle's own BYOL support. They're unsure which licenses transfer to which AWS services.
questions.question: Which statements about license portability are CORRECT?
choices: SQL Server BYOL works on EC2 and RDS; Oracle BYOL works on EC2 and RDS for Oracle (with limitations). DB2 doesn't have a managed AWS service — EC2 BYOL only. Oracle on RDS requires specific BYOL license types (Database Enterprise/Standard Edition 2) and the 'license included' option may be more cost-effective for certain workloads. Microsoft License Mobility generally allows SQL Server Enterprise BYOL to dedicated hosts or certain instance types; standard edition BYOL has different rules.
All commercial database licenses transfer freely to any AWS service.
Only Oracle licenses can BYOL; all other databases require purchasing AWS licenses.
Database licenses don't transfer to AWS — the company must purchase all new licenses.
questions.explanation:
This question tests actual license knowledge that experienced architects often get wrong because it's ugly and vendor-specific.

SQL Server: License Mobility allows SQL Server Enterprise BYOL on shared-tenancy EC2 or RDS. SQL Server Standard has more restrictive mobility — may require dedicated hosts for certain scenarios. License Included is often cheaper for smaller deployments.

Oracle: BYOL supported on EC2 and RDS for Oracle (with specific edition requirements — Database Enterprise Edition, Standard Edition 2). Oracle licenses count differently on virtualized infrastructure — the exam doesn't test specific counting rules but tests that you know this is complicated.

DB2: No RDS support; EC2 BYOL only, plus IBM licensing rules apply.

Option B is wrong (licensing has restrictions). Option C is wrong (multiple vendors support BYOL). Option D is wrong (BYOL options exist).

The subtle exam value: if a scenario asks 'can we BYOL this SQL Server to RDS,' the answer is yes but with edition and mobility considerations. The exam often has 'use License Included' as a trap when BYOL would save money, or 'BYOL on EC2' as a trap when RDS supports it.
questions.pattern: Database licensing on AWS is vendor-specific. SQL Server: BYOL or License Included on RDS, License Mobility rules for EC2. Oracle: BYOL on EC2 and RDS (editions matter). DB2: EC2 only. Always consider license cost AND operational burden — License Included on RDS removes license management overhead even at premium pricing.

questions.scenario: A security architect wants to implement a 'least privilege baseline' across 50 accounts: every IAM role should have permission boundaries preventing escalation. However, they notice that their Terraform-created roles have boundaries applied inconsistently — some don't have them. They want a mechanism that forcibly applies a boundary when any role is created, regardless of whether the creator included it.
questions.question: Which approach MOST effectively enforces mandatory permission boundaries?
choices: IAM permission boundaries can be mandatory via an SCP: the SCP denies iam:CreateRole and iam:CreateUser unless the request includes a specific aws:RequestTag or iam:PermissionsBoundary condition matching the required boundary ARN.
Use AWS Config auto-remediation to add permission boundaries after detection.
Require all IAM roles to be created through AWS Service Catalog, which can enforce boundaries.
IAM permission boundaries cannot be made mandatory; educate developers instead.
questions.pattern: Enforcing permission boundaries: SCP with iam:PermissionsBoundary condition on CreateRole/CreateUser. Same pattern applies to iam:PermissionsBoundary for iam:PutRolePermissionsBoundary (preventing removal). This is the canonical 'boundaries are actually enforced, not aspirational' implementation.

references.title: Delegating IAM administration with permission boundaries

questions.scenario: A company's API uses API Gateway → Lambda → RDS Proxy → RDS. At high load, they see 'Too Many Connections' errors from RDS despite using RDS Proxy. Investigation shows Lambda concurrent executions spike to 2000+ during bursts. RDS Proxy is configured with a max connection pool of 200 and pinned connections for each Lambda invocation due to the code using TEMPORARY tables.
questions.question: What is the ROOT CAUSE and the correct fix?
choices: Increase RDS Proxy max connections to 2000 to match Lambda concurrency.

The root cause is connection pinning: when Lambda code uses session-state features (TEMPORARY tables, session variables, prepared statements in some configurations), RDS Proxy 'pins' that connection to the Lambda invocation and cannot multiplex. With 2000 concurrent Lambdas each holding pinned connections, the pool is exhausted. Fix: remove session-state dependencies in Lambda code (use real tables instead of TEMPORARY, parameterize queries without prepared-statement caching) so RDS Proxy can multiplex connections across invocations.
Configure Lambda reserved concurrency to match RDS Proxy pool size (200).
Switch to RDS Proxy with persistent connections for Lambda.
questions.explanation:
Connection pinning is the hidden failure mode of RDS Proxy. The Proxy's value comes from multiplexing — one backend connection serving many client connections. When a client uses session-state (TEMPORARY tables, session variables, SET commands, prepared statements with certain configurations), the Proxy must pin the backend connection to that client for correctness. With many pinned clients, the pool exhausts.

Option A (raise pool to 2000) defeats the purpose of RDS Proxy and still stresses RDS. Option C (reserved concurrency) limits Lambda to 200 concurrent, throttling the API unnecessarily. Option D (persistent connections) doesn't exist as an option for Lambda+Proxy.

Fix options: (1) remove session-state features from Lambda code, (2) use persistent non-pinned patterns (standard tables, no SET variables), (3) accept higher concurrency by scaling RDS Proxy and RDS.

The subtle exam value: RDS Proxy is often positioned as 'the fix for Lambda + RDS connection exhaustion,' but it has this specific gotcha. CloudWatch metric DatabaseConnectionsCurrentlyBeingPinned surfaces this — the exam may test whether you know to check it.
questions.pattern: RDS Proxy pinning: TEMPORARY tables, session variables, certain prepared statements, SET commands → connection pins to client → multiplexing benefit lost. Check DatabaseConnectionsCurrentlyBeingPinned CloudWatch metric. Fix by rewriting Lambda code to avoid session-state, not by growing the pool.

questions.scenario: A team runs a batch ML training workload on EC2 p4d.24xlarge (GPU) instances using Spot. Jobs take 8-20 hours; checkpoints are written every 30 minutes. They notice Spot interruption rates have climbed over the past 3 months, with some jobs getting interrupted 4-5 times — delaying completion beyond SLA. Monthly compute spend is $80,000 on Spot. The team wants to reduce interruption impact without losing Spot savings entirely.
questions.question: Which optimization MOST effectively addresses the interruption problem?
choices: Switch entirely to On-Demand p4d.24xlarge to eliminate interruptions.

Use Spot with capacity-optimized allocation strategy across multiple instance types (p4d.24xlarge, p4de.24xlarge, p5.48xlarge with appropriate capacity weights) and multiple AZs. Use Spot placement scores or the EC2 Spot placement score API to identify low-interruption capacity pools. Consider Spot Blocks (if still available in your region) for critical jobs; otherwise migrate time-sensitive jobs to On-Demand and keep Spot for flexible workloads.
Increase checkpoint frequency to every 5 minutes to reduce re-run cost per interruption.
Use SageMaker Managed Spot Training, which promises zero interruptions.
questions.explanation:
Multiple correct-ish answers; B is the most complete.

Capacity-optimized allocation chooses pools with most available capacity — directly reduces interruption rate. Diversifying across instance types (p4d, p4de, p5) and AZs increases pool availability. Spot placement scores help predict pool stability before launching.

Option A (all On-Demand) eliminates interruptions but at ~70% higher cost — contradicts 'without losing Spot savings.' Option C (frequent checkpoints) reduces re-run cost but doesn't reduce interruption rate; also adds I/O overhead. Option D (Managed Spot Training) is MISLEADING — SageMaker Managed Spot still uses Spot and still has interruptions; it just handles them automatically. 'Zero interruptions' is not true.

The subtle trap: 'Managed Spot Training' sounds like a silver bullet; it isn't. It just manages interruption recovery automatically. The fundamental way to reduce interruption rate is diversification and pool selection.

Spot Blocks (fixed-duration Spot with guaranteed capacity for 1-6 hours) existed historically but AWS has deprecated them in most scenarios — verify availability at exam time.
questions.pattern: Spot interruption reduction: capacity-optimized allocation (not lowest-price), diversify instance types + AZs, use Spot placement scores, and verify the specific workload actually tolerates interruption in practice. GPU Spot specifically tends to be more volatile than CPU Spot.

questions.scenario: A company's hybrid setup has on-premises DNS servers that resolve corp.example.internal zones. They're moving workloads to AWS VPCs that need to resolve both corp.example.internal (on-prem) and *.aws.example.internal (Route 53 private hosted zones). They also need on-prem resolvers to resolve AWS private zones. Currently using conditional forwarders on on-prem DNS. They want to consolidate DNS resolution management.
questions.question: Which Route 53 configuration MOST effectively handles bidirectional hybrid DNS?
choices: Use Route 53 public hosted zones for aws.example.internal; on-prem configures standard DNS lookups.

Use Route 53 Resolver: create OUTBOUND endpoints in VPCs that forward corp.example.internal queries to on-prem DNS servers. Create INBOUND endpoints in VPCs that on-prem DNS can forward aws.example.internal queries to. Configure Resolver Rules for the forwarding. Use Resolver Rules sharing via RAM if multiple VPCs need the same rules.
Run EC2 BIND servers in a VPC and point everything at them.
Use AWS Directory Service which includes DNS forwarding.
questions.explanation:
Route 53 Resolver is AWS's purpose-built service for hybrid DNS. The standard architecture:

- Outbound endpoints: VPCs forward queries for specific domains (corp.example.internal) to on-prem
- Inbound endpoints: on-prem DNS forwards queries for specific domains (aws.example.internal) to the VPC's Resolver
- Resolver Rules: define which domains forward where; shareable via RAM for multi-VPC consistency

Option A (public hosted zones) exposes internal names publicly — bad practice. Option C (self-managed BIND) reintroduces exactly the operational burden Route 53 Resolver eliminates. Option D (Directory Service) is for AD/LDAP, not general DNS forwarding.

The subtle exam value: many architects use on-prem DNS with conditional forwarders pointing at the VPC's built-in resolver (169.254.169.253). That works for outbound (VPC → on-prem) but NOT for inbound (on-prem → VPC) because 169.254.169.253 is only accessible from within the VPC. Inbound endpoints are required for on-prem → VPC resolution.
questions.pattern: Hybrid DNS with Route 53 Resolver: OUTBOUND endpoints for VPC → on-prem queries, INBOUND endpoints for on-prem → VPC queries. The 169.254.169.253 VPC resolver is ONLY reachable from within the VPC — not from on-prem. RAM-sharing Resolver Rules for multi-VPC.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating a 50 TB on-premises file share (SMB) used by Windows clients and some Linux clients. Migration target is cloud-based file access with: full SMB protocol support, POSIX compliance for Linux clients, low-latency access from AWS-hosted applications, and direct internet access from remote offices without VPN where possible.
questions.question: Which target architecture BEST fits?
choices: S3 with Storage Gateway File Gateway on-prem for SMB access and S3 API for Linux clients.

Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP — supports SMB, NFS (POSIX), and iSCSI simultaneously on the same file system. Includes in-place storage efficiency (dedup/compression) and SnapMirror replication. Linux clients use NFS; Windows clients use SMB; no protocol translation needed.
Amazon EFS for both Windows and Linux clients using the EFS Windows client.
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server plus Amazon EFS in parallel, synchronizing files between them.
questions.explanation:
FSx for NetApp ONTAP (FSxN) is the right answer specifically because it supports multi-protocol access on the SAME file system — SMB for Windows, NFS for Linux POSIX, iSCSI for block. No protocol translation, no dual-storage-with-sync complexity. It also has NetApp's storage efficiency features and SnapMirror for replication.

Option A (File Gateway + S3) doesn't give low-latency cloud-native file access — you'd need File Gateway in AWS VPCs. Option C (EFS for Windows) — EFS's Windows client support is limited and typically not production-recommended. Option D (dual systems with sync) is expensive and operationally painful.

The subtle exam value: FSx has four flavors — Windows File Server (Windows-only SMB), Lustre (HPC), NetApp ONTAP (multi-protocol), OpenZFS (NFS-focused). Multi-protocol requirement specifically picks NetApp ONTAP; Windows-only picks Windows File Server; HPC/ML data throughput picks Lustre.
questions.pattern: FSx family selection: Windows File Server (SMB only, AD-integrated), Lustre (HPC, high throughput), NetApp ONTAP (multi-protocol SMB+NFS+iSCSI), OpenZFS (NFS, snapshots, cloning). Multi-protocol requirements → NetApp ONTAP. EFS is NFS-only; its Windows support is limited.

references.title: FSx for NetApp ONTAP use cases

questions.scenario: A financial services company has a standing need to send data from 10 AWS accounts (in their Organization) to 5 external partner AWS accounts (NOT in their Organization). Current approach: S3 bucket replication with bucket policies granting partner access. This has caused security incidents when partner IAM roles were compromised and attackers accessed shared data. Compliance now requires: data in transit between the company and partners must be auditable and revocable at a single point, with network-level traffic isolation.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST meets these requirements?
choices: Continue S3 replication but add MFA-required bucket policies for partner access.

Replace S3 bucket sharing with AWS PrivateLink from each company account exposing partner-specific endpoint services. Partners connect via their own PrivateLink interface endpoints. Each partner connection has its own endpoint service with individual policies, so revoking one partner doesn't affect others. All traffic flows over AWS backbone, never internet.
Create IAM roles in each company account that partners assume via cross-account trust; rotate trust annually.
Use AWS Transfer Family with partner-specific credentials for each partner.
questions.explanation:
PrivateLink is the correct answer for 'secure cross-account data exchange without internet exposure.' Each partner gets their own endpoint service, which means:
- Revoking one partner: delete or update that specific endpoint service
- Network-level isolation: traffic flows over AWS backbone via endpoints, not public internet
- Auditable: CloudTrail logs endpoint service connections and usage
- Per-partner policies: endpoint service permissions granted per-connection

Option A (S3 + MFA) still has the fundamental issue of broad bucket access. Option C (cross-account roles) has the same bearer-token compromise risk as the original. Option D (Transfer Family) is for SFTP/FTPS file exchange — different use case.

The subtle exam value: PrivateLink is often positioned as 'VPC to VPC' connectivity, but its cross-account / cross-organization use case is equally important. For partner data exchange specifically, PrivateLink with endpoint services per partner is a stronger security posture than shared S3 buckets.
questions.pattern: Cross-account secure data exchange patterns: S3 cross-account (broad, role-based, internet-optional) → PrivateLink endpoint services (narrow, network-isolated, per-partner). When 'revocable at single point' or 'network isolation' is in scenario, PrivateLink wins over S3 sharing.

questions.scenario: A team migrated a web application from EC2 to ECS Fargate 6 months ago. Performance has been good but costs are 30% higher than their EC2 baseline. They're considering migrating to EKS, to Lambda, or back to EC2. The application runs 24/7 (not intermittent), averages 15% CPU utilization during off-hours and 75% during peak, serves HTTP requests with typical request duration of 50-200ms.
questions.question: Which cost-optimization path BEST fits this workload?
