choices: Migrate back to EC2 Auto Scaling groups with Compute Savings Plans — Fargate premium isn't justified for steady 24/7 workloads.
Migrate to Lambda since the request duration is short.

Stay on Fargate but purchase Compute Savings Plans (which cover Fargate), add Fargate Spot for non-critical capacity, and investigate Fargate task CPU/memory right-sizing — Fargate's operational savings often outweigh the compute premium.
Migrate to EKS to get Kubernetes flexibility.
questions.explanation:
This question forces honest tradeoff reasoning. Fargate IS more expensive per compute-unit than EC2. But total cost = compute cost + operational cost. For teams already running Fargate successfully, going back to EC2 reintroduces: AMI management, capacity planning, instance patching, security groups at the instance level, ASG tuning.

Option A: valid on paper; in practice, the 30% compute premium often costs less than the operational burden of EC2. Only right if the team has dedicated capacity for EC2 ops.

Option B (Lambda): 50-200ms × 24/7 × sustained load = Lambda is usually more expensive than Fargate/EC2 for this scale. Lambda wins for spiky/low-utilization workloads.

Option C: Compute Savings Plans cover Fargate. Fargate Spot gives ~70% discount on Fargate pricing for fault-tolerant workloads. Right-sizing Fargate task definitions (CPU/memory) is often overlooked — teams over-provision task resources. Combining these can close the 30% gap.

Option D (EKS): adds Kubernetes operational burden; doesn't inherently reduce cost unless combined with EC2 worker nodes.

The subtle exam value: the 'correct' answer depends on factors NOT in the scenario (team size, ops skill). The exam rewards identifying Compute SPs + Fargate Spot + right-sizing as the Fargate-specific cost levers.
questions.pattern: Fargate cost optimization: Compute Savings Plans (yes, Fargate is covered), Fargate Spot for fault-tolerant tasks, task right-sizing (CPU/memory), and minimum container counts. Going 'back to EC2' only wins if operational cost savings exceed the compute premium — often not the case for smaller teams.

references.title: AWS Fargate pricing and Savings Plans

questions.scenario: A team's serverless API (API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB) experiences intermittent 502 errors affecting ~0.5% of requests during peak load. CloudWatch shows Lambda function duration spikes to 29 seconds before errors (the function's configured timeout is 30 seconds). The function normally completes in 80-300ms. DynamoDB metrics show no throttling. Lambda concurrency is well below account limits.
questions.question: What is the MOST LIKELY root cause and fix?
choices: Lambda is hitting memory limits; increase memory allocation.

Most likely cause: Lambda cold starts are long for affected requests (new containers initializing), possibly exacerbated by VPC ENI attachment if the function is in a VPC. Fix: enable Provisioned Concurrency for the critical Lambda, and if it's in a VPC, verify VPC networking isn't adding cold-start time (VPC Lambda cold starts were historically slow; now much better but still non-zero). Also check for init-heavy code (large imports, DB connection establishment).
DynamoDB is throttling silently; enable adaptive capacity.
API Gateway is the bottleneck; upgrade to HTTP API from REST API.
questions.explanation:
The pattern — 80-300ms normal duration but occasional 29-second spikes hitting timeout — is the signature of cold starts. When Lambda doesn't have a warm container available, it must initialize a new one: download code, attach ENI (if VPC), run init handlers, establish connections. For some functions, this takes seconds.

Fix pieces:
- **Provisioned Concurrency** keeps N containers always warm. Guaranteed no cold starts for that baseline.
- **VPC ENI speedup**: AWS improved VPC Lambda cold starts in 2019 (Hyperplane ENIs), but VPC-attached Lambdas still have some overhead. Non-VPC Lambdas start fastest.
- **Init code review**: heavy imports (pandas, tensorflow) or connection establishment in init code adds seconds.

Option A (memory): memory issues don't cause 29-second hangs; they cause OOM errors. Option C (DynamoDB throttling): scenario explicitly says no throttling. Option D (HTTP API vs REST): HTTP API is faster, but not the cause of 29-second spikes.

The subtle trap: 502 errors are API Gateway's way of reporting 'backend failure,' which can mean Lambda timeout, Lambda error, or Lambda unresponsive. 29-second duration right before timeout strongly suggests timeout-due-to-cold-start.
questions.pattern: Lambda 502 at API Gateway with max-duration spikes = cold start. Fixes: Provisioned Concurrency for guaranteed warm, init-code optimization, consider non-VPC Lambda if possible, or SnapStart for Java. Don't confuse 502 (Lambda timeout/error) with 504 (API Gateway timeout). 502s with max-duration pattern = cold start specifically.

questions.scenario: A company's mainframe (IBM z/OS) runs a 30-year-old COBOL application processing insurance claims. The mainframe lease ends in 18 months. The company has assessed modernization: COBOL-to-Java conversion via AWS Mainframe Modernization's Blu Age tool estimates 14 months; AWS Mainframe Modernization's Micro Focus runtime estimates 9 months as a rehost. Complete rewrite by AWS Professional Services estimates 24 months. Business wants to be off mainframe by the lease end.
questions.question: Which modernization approach BEST fits the timeline?
choices: Micro Focus rehost via AWS Mainframe Modernization — keeps COBOL code running on AWS-hosted Micro Focus runtime on EC2, 9-month estimated timeline fits within constraints. Modernization to Java can happen later.
Blu Age refactor (COBOL → Java) via AWS Mainframe Modernization — 14 months, within window, results in modern Java code.
Complete rewrite by AWS ProServe — 24 months, exceeds window.
Extend the mainframe lease to avoid rushing modernization.
questions.explanation:
With 18 months available and the Blu Age timeline estimated at 14 months (very close to the deadline with no buffer for integration testing, user acceptance, data migration), the risk is high. Micro Focus rehost at 9 months provides meaningful buffer for the things that always take longer than expected.

This is a 'picking the right R' question. Rehost (Micro Focus) gets you off mainframe fast; refactor (Blu Age) produces better long-term artifacts but requires runway. For a hard deadline, rehost first, refactor later.

Option B is tempting — refactored Java is clearly better long-term. But 14/18 months leaves 4 months of buffer for a project with 30 years of accumulated complexity. Mainframe migrations routinely exceed estimates.

Option C (rewrite) exceeds deadline. Option D (extend lease) may not be possible business-wise.

The subtle exam value: this tests whether you pick pragmatic Rehost over aspirational Refactor when timeline is constrained. It also tests knowledge of AWS Mainframe Modernization service specifically — this is an underused service that SAP-C02 exam may test.
questions.pattern: AWS Mainframe Modernization has two runtime patterns: Micro Focus (rehost, keep COBOL, fastest) and Blu Age (refactor, COBOL→Java, slower but produces modern code). Tight deadline → Micro Focus. Runway for modernization → Blu Age. Mainframe projects ALWAYS slip; always build buffer.

references.title: Mainframe Modernization runtimes (Micro Focus vs Blu Age)

questions.scenario: A company requires customer-managed KMS keys for all data encryption across 70 accounts. Keys must be: (a) per-business-unit ownership (not shared across BUs), (b) rotatable without breaking existing encrypted data, (c) replicable to a DR region, (d) manageable through IaC. The current approach uses AWS-managed KMS keys, which don't satisfy requirements (a) or (c).
questions.question: Which key architecture MOST effectively meets these requirements?
choices: Customer-managed KMS keys with automatic key rotation enabled; use Multi-Region Keys for DR region replication. Each BU gets its own set of keys with IAM policies scoping usage to that BU's workloads. Terraform/CloudFormation defines keys.
Use AWS CloudHSM for all encryption to meet all requirements.
Customer-managed KMS keys; for DR, manually re-encrypt data with new keys in the DR region when failover occurs.
Use AWS KMS external key store for all keys to maintain full control.
questions.explanation:
This question tests specific KMS capabilities:

- **Customer-managed keys** (vs AWS-managed): required for (a) per-BU ownership and key policies.
- **Automatic key rotation**: rotates the key material annually without breaking existing encrypted data (KMS stores multiple key versions and knows which version encrypted each ciphertext).
- **Multi-Region Keys**: KMS feature that replicates a key to other regions with the same key ID and material — crucial for DR because encrypted data can be decrypted in the DR region without re-encryption.
- **IaC**: both Terraform and CloudFormation support KMS key resources.

Option B (CloudHSM): heavy operational lift; not needed unless FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or specific compliance demands it.

Option C (manual re-encryption on failover): catastrophic operational burden; defeats DR automation.

Option D (external key store): useful for 'keep keys on-premises' scenarios; overkill here and adds latency.

The subtle exam value: **Multi-Region Keys** is a KMS feature many architects don't know exists. Without it, cross-region encrypted data requires re-encryption, which is operationally painful. With it, the SAME key ID works in multiple regions.
questions.pattern: KMS key types: AWS-managed (no policy control, per-service), customer-managed (full control, rotatable), multi-Region keys (same key ID across regions, critical for DR), external key store (key material outside AWS). DR + encryption at rest → Multi-Region Keys. Automatic rotation: annual, transparent to applications.

questions.scenario: A retail company has 5 recently-acquired subsidiaries, each with their own AWS estates: 20 accounts total across them. None are currently in the acquiring company's Organization. The acquiring company wants: unified cost visibility across all 25 accounts (5 subsidiaries + 20 existing), centralized security findings, consistent tagging — all within 90 days. The acquiring company also wants the ability to roll back if the M&A deal faces regulatory issues (rare but possible).
questions.question: Which onboarding approach BEST balances the requirements?
choices: Immediately invite all 20 subsidiary accounts into the acquiring company's Organization for maximum integration speed.

Create a new Organization for M&A-in-progress accounts with the acquiring company as management. Use AWS Organizations' 'invite' flow to move subsidiary accounts in. Enable Cost Explorer cross-account analysis, delegated Security Hub administration, and tagging policies. Accounts can be 'removed' from the Organization (reverting to standalone) if the deal falls through — reversible migration.
Wait for legal resolution before any integration.
Use AWS Control Tower account factory to provision new accounts and manually migrate data from subsidiary accounts.
questions.explanation:
M&A scenarios have a specific pattern: rapid integration for operational benefits AND reversibility for deal risk.

Key capabilities:
- AWS Organizations account invitation: non-destructive, reversible
- Account removal from Organization: returns account to standalone status; most resources intact
- Cost Explorer cross-account: immediate financial visibility
- Security Hub delegated admin: centralized findings
- Tagging policies via Organizations: consistent tagging

Option A (immediate full integration) is directionally right but lacks the planning for reversibility.

Option C (wait for legal) fails the 90-day requirement and wastes optionality.

Option D (new accounts + data migration) is destructive — if the deal falls through, you can't easily undo account creation and data copies.

The subtle trap: the answer must address BOTH operational integration AND the reversibility requirement. Any answer that only addresses integration misses the M&A-specific nuance. Inviting accounts is reversible; rebuilding is not.
questions.pattern: M&A integration pattern: invite subsidiary accounts to Organization (reversible), enable cross-account tools (Cost Explorer, Security Hub, Config aggregator, tagging policies), document integration steps for potential reversal. Don't rebuild or migrate data prematurely; join Organization first, migrate workloads later.

questions.scenario:
GlobalShip Logistics is a 40-year-old freight company headquartered in Rotterdam with operations in 23 countries. They recently appointed a new CIO from a tech-first competitor who is driving digital transformation. The company's IT estate includes a mix of on-premises VMware (about 1,200 VMs in their primary Rotterdam data center and 400 in a secondary Amsterdam facility), a Microsoft Azure deployment for their new customer portal, and some older AWS usage from an acquired subsidiary (Accounts in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 managed separately from corporate IT).

The flagship application to migrate is their Tariff Calculation Engine (TCE), a 15-year-old .NET Framework 4.6 application running on 18 Windows Server 2016 VMs, backed by a SQL Server 2017 Enterprise 6 TB database with Always On Availability Groups across the two data centers. TCE processes approximately 2.4 million tariff calculations daily with seasonal peaks during Q4 (3× normal volume). The application uses SQL Server Service Broker for async queueing between stored procedures, Windows-authenticated connections from internal applications, and SQL CLR assemblies for specialized math functions developed in C# over the past decade.

The business has approved a 14-month migration timeline, a budget of €8M for the migration itself, and wants to 'modernize where sensible' without open-ended refactoring. The CISO has specified that the new architecture must support audit logging per ISO 27001 requirements (already in place on-premises) and that customer tariff data cannot leave the EU. The CIO publicly committed to '50% infrastructure cost reduction within 24 months of migration' in a recent press interview.

The CTO of the acquired subsidiary has offered to lead the migration, proposing to refactor the entire TCE to .NET 8 on Linux containers in EKS with Aurora PostgreSQL using Babelfish for Aurora PostgreSQL for SQL Server compatibility.
questions.question: Which migration approach BEST fits the actual constraints in the scenario?
choices: Accept the CTO's refactor proposal. Babelfish provides SQL Server compatibility, .NET 8 on Linux is modern, and EKS gives container flexibility. This achieves maximum modernization and meets the CIO's cost-reduction commitment.

Rehost to EC2 using AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) for the 18 Windows VMs. Use RDS for SQL Server Enterprise (BYOL from existing licensing) in eu-west-1 for data residency. Migrate Always On AGs to RDS Multi-AZ. Plan modernization as a post-migration phase.
Use Azure Migrate since the company already has Azure presence; consolidate onto Azure for operational simplicity.
Replatform using AWS Elastic Beanstalk for the .NET application with RDS for SQL Server, achieving 'modernize where sensible' via managed runtime without code changes.
questions.explanation:
The noise in this scenario is substantial — 40 years old, 23 countries, new CIO, acquired subsidiary, Rotterdam, press interview, €8M budget, Q4 peaks — most of it irrelevant to the actual architectural decision.

The real constraints that matter:
1. SQL CLR assemblies — Babelfish doesn't support CLR. Refactor answer (A) breaks.
2. Service Broker — Babelfish doesn't support. Refactor (A) breaks again.
3. 14-month timeline + 'modernize where sensible' + 'no open-ended refactoring' — argues against aggressive refactor.
4. Data residency (EU only) — any target works if deployed in eu-west-1.
5. Existing SQL Server Enterprise licenses — BYOL opportunity.
6. Windows Authentication — RDS for SQL Server supports this; may not work on Aurora.

Option A (refactor to Babelfish): Babelfish compatibility gap is a deal-breaker. The CTO's proposal sounds great but violates hard constraints. Common trap: the internal advocate's plan.

Option B (rehost): respects all hard constraints. Modernization happens later when the compatibility gaps can be addressed.

Option C (Azure): the scenario mentions Azure as noise — the company has Azure for ONE thing (customer portal). That doesn't mean consolidating there is right, especially since the question is an AWS certification context and there's no architectural reason to switch platforms.

Option D (Beanstalk): Beanstalk supports .NET on Windows, but the underlying SQL Server CLR and Service Broker dependencies still exist — Beanstalk doesn't magically make those work elsewhere. The 'managed runtime' framing is tempting but doesn't solve the actual problems.

The CIO's press commitment (50% cost reduction) is pure noise. It sounds important but doesn't change the technical architecture — it's a business-political consideration.
questions.pattern: Long-scenario discipline: (1) read the question first, (2) identify hard technical constraints (SQL CLR, Service Broker, Windows Auth — feature dependencies), (3) identify data/compliance constraints (EU residency), (4) ignore business-political noise (press commitments, new CIO, org history), (5) eliminate options that violate hard constraints. Advocate-named solutions in scenarios (the CTO's proposal here) are frequently wrong — red flag.

references.title: Babelfish compatibility with SQL Server features
RDS for SQL Server — CLR and Service Broker support

questions.scenario:
MedStream Analytics is building a patient monitoring platform for hospital ICUs. The system ingests vital-sign data from approximately 50,000 bedside monitors across 180 partner hospitals in North America. Each monitor emits a JSON message every 2 seconds containing heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, and several other measurements. The platform also ingests periodic lab results from hospital EHR systems (HL7 FHIR format, arriving in bursts of 500–10,000 messages every 15 minutes).

Clinicians access the data through a web dashboard showing near-real-time patient status, with alerts fired when specific thresholds are breached (e.g., tachycardia, hypotension). Alert latency is critical — end-to-end from sensor emission to alert delivery must be under 10 seconds at p99. Clinicians expect to see dashboard values within 5 seconds of the underlying measurement. Historical data is used for trend analysis and is retained for 7 years per HIPAA requirements.

The current architecture, designed 3 years ago by a contractor who has since left the company, uses Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a shard count of 32, Lambda consumers that perform threshold evaluation, and DynamoDB for current-state storage with a TTL of 72 hours. Historical data lands in S3 via a separate Kinesis Data Firehose branch. Alerts are delivered via SNS to hospital pager systems. The team's SRE lead noticed that during Q4 respiratory season (November-January), Kinesis throttling occurs intermittently — specific shards get overwhelmed while others are underutilized. CloudWatch shows IteratorAge spiking to 45+ seconds on affected shards, violating the 10-second alert SLA.

A newly-hired architect proposes migrating ingestion from Kinesis to Amazon MSK, citing Kafka's better partition rebalancing. The CTO is hesitant about the operational burden of managed Kafka. The VP of Engineering asks whether Kinesis can be tuned to solve this.
questions.question: Which approach MOST effectively addresses the root cause?
choices: Migrate to Amazon MSK as the new architect proposes. Kafka's consumer group rebalancing inherently handles uneven partition load better than Kinesis.

The root cause is hot shards — specific hospitals likely send data with partition keys that hash to the same shards, causing uneven distribution. Fix: change the partition key strategy to better distribute (e.g., include a random suffix, or use UUID-based keys with explicit hash-based distribution). If per-hospital ordering is required, use a composite key that distributes across shards while maintaining per-hospital order via sequencing.
Increase Kinesis shard count from 32 to 128 to spread load across more shards.
Migrate to Kinesis Data Streams on-demand mode, which auto-scales shards based on load.
questions.explanation:
This scenario is loaded with noise: hospital count, vital signs enumeration, HL7 FHIR, contractor history, HIPAA retention, architect-vs-CTO politics. The real symptom is buried: **IteratorAge spiking on specific shards while others underutilize = classic hot-shard pattern from poor partition key distribution.**

Option A (MSK migration): MSK has similar partition-key issues — Kafka also distributes by key hash. Rebalancing in Kafka occurs when consumers join/leave; it doesn't fix skewed PRODUCER distribution. The migration is operational overhead without fixing the root cause.

Option B (partition key redesign): the correct diagnosis. If all sensors from one hospital use the hospital ID as partition key, that hospital's traffic hits one shard regardless of total shard count. A composite key (hospital_id + bed_id + random_suffix, or hospital_id# with proper distribution) spreads the load. If per-patient ordering is required, use patient_id as partition key (many patients = many partition values = natural distribution).

Option C (more shards): doesn't help if one hospital's key still hashes to one shard. Also doubles cost.

Option D (on-demand mode): Kinesis on-demand auto-scales total throughput, but a single partition key still hits one logical shard. On-demand helps with overall scale, not hot-key problems.

The noise successfully obscures the diagnosis: respiratory season, ICU drama, contractor who left. None of that tells you the shard is hot for KEY DISTRIBUTION reasons. The CloudWatch detail (IteratorAge on specific shards while others underutilized) is the actual diagnostic signal.
questions.pattern: Kinesis hot shard diagnosis: if IteratorAge is high on SPECIFIC shards while others are low, partition key distribution is the problem — not shard count. Fixes: change partition key to distribute better, or accept per-key latency for ordering guarantees. On-demand mode fixes aggregate scale, not key-distribution skew. MSK migration doesn't fix producer-side skew.

references.title: Kinesis Data Streams — resharding and key distribution

questions.scenario:
Prothera Financial is a mid-sized wealth management firm serving high-net-worth clients in the US, UK, and Singapore. Founded in 1998 and recently acquired by a larger private equity firm, Prothera operates 47 AWS accounts managed through AWS Organizations with all features enabled. They were an early AWS customer (since 2011) and have accumulated technical debt in their Org structure — the top-level OU structure doesn't match current business units, and some accounts are in the root OU because historical moves were never completed.

The new parent PE firm has mandated unified security posture across its portfolio companies within 90 days. Prothera's CISO has identified three immediate priorities: (1) every account must have AWS Config enabled with a specific set of 34 managed rules evaluating compliance with the parent firm's baseline; (2) GuardDuty must be enabled in all accounts and regions; (3) detailed CloudTrail logs from all accounts must flow to a central Logging account, retained for 7 years with tamper-evident controls.

The current state: Config is enabled in 31 of 47 accounts, with inconsistent rule sets. GuardDuty is enabled in 22 accounts, with different detectors across regions. CloudTrail management events are logged per-account to per-account S3 buckets; there's no central aggregation. The parent firm uses AWS Control Tower in their own environment but Prothera's existing multi-account structure predates Control Tower.

The CTO is evaluating two options: (A) enroll all 47 Prothera accounts into a new Control Tower landing zone, migrating workloads to the new account factory accounts over time; or (B) keep existing accounts and apply the required baselines via Organization-level service configurations (Config organizational rules, GuardDuty delegated admin with auto-enable, CloudTrail organization trail). The junior security engineer favors option A because 'Control Tower is the best practice.' The platform lead favors option B because 'we can't disrupt 47 accounts in 90 days.'
questions.question: Which approach MEETS the 90-day mandate while MINIMIZING disruption?
choices: Option A — enroll all accounts into a new Control Tower landing zone. The migration will be complete within 90 days using the account factory.

Option B — apply Organization-level service configurations: enable AWS Config with Organizational Rules and deploy the 34 required rules via Config conformance packs; use GuardDuty delegated admin with auto-enable-for-new-accounts; create an Organization CloudTrail trail with a central S3 bucket in the Logging account with Object Lock for tamper-evidence. This meets the mandate without requiring workload migration.
Hybrid: enroll only NEW accounts into Control Tower; leave the existing 47 as-is indefinitely.
Neither option; recommend a 6-month project to Control Tower-ify everything properly.
questions.explanation:
The scenario is loaded with noise: founded 1998, PE acquisition, OU structure drift, early AWS customer, regional spread. The hard constraints buried inside:
- 90-day deadline (non-negotiable)
- 47 existing accounts with workloads (disruption risk)
- Three specific security requirements (Config with 34 rules, GuardDuty org-wide, CloudTrail centralized with 7-year tamper-evident retention)

Option A (Control Tower enrollment): Control Tower CAN enroll existing accounts, but migrating 47 accounts' workloads to new account factory accounts in 90 days is implausible. Also, Control Tower has guardrails that may conflict with existing configurations, requiring remediation. The junior engineer's 'Control Tower is best practice' is the kind of advocate-opinion that's often wrong under time pressure.

Option B (Org-level services): hits every requirement without touching workloads.
- Config Organizational Rules + conformance packs: deploy the 34 rules across all accounts from one place.
- GuardDuty delegated admin with auto-enable: org-wide enablement.
- Organization CloudTrail trail + S3 Object Lock in Logging account: centralized tamper-evident logs.

This is achievable in 90 days and meets every stated requirement.

Option C (hybrid new-only): doesn't cover the 47 existing accounts, failing the mandate.

Option D (6-month proper project): violates 90-day mandate.

The subtle trap: Control Tower is the 'pristine greenfield' answer. For existing multi-account environments with deadlines, direct use of Organizations' governance services is usually faster and less risky. You don't HAVE to use Control Tower to get good governance.
questions.pattern: Governance at scale without Control Tower: Organizations + delegated admin (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config) + Organization trails (CloudTrail) + RAM-shared resources. Control Tower is optional — it packages these into a landing zone but the underlying services can be used directly. Existing environments with deadlines favor direct-service approach over Control Tower enrollment.

references.title: GuardDuty multi-account management with delegated admin

questions.scenario:
RetailPulse operates a flash-sale e-commerce platform. Customer acquisition spikes 40× during flash sale events (which run 3–5 times per month, each lasting 90 minutes). Outside flash sales, traffic is roughly 500 RPS; during flash sales, traffic hits 20,000 RPS. The platform's core services run on ECS Fargate behind an Application Load Balancer, with DynamoDB for product catalog and user sessions, and RDS for PostgreSQL (db.r6g.4xlarge, Multi-AZ) for order processing.

Three years ago, the team built a custom 'warm-up' system — a scheduled Lambda that pre-scales ECS services and triggers RDS read replica creation 30 minutes before each announced flash sale. This system works but requires the marketing team to enter sale start times into a custom web form, and missed pre-warming has caused two outages in the past year when marketing failed to enter times. The CTO wants to eliminate the manual coordination.

The order-processing RDS instance has been the primary bottleneck — CPU spikes to 95%+ during sales, write latency climbs from 3ms baseline to 120ms+. The DBA has tried vertical scaling to db.r6g.8xlarge but the cost of running the larger instance continuously is prohibitive. A proposal was made to migrate to Aurora PostgreSQL, but the application uses specific PostgreSQL extensions (pg_cron for job scheduling, plpython3u for custom functions) and the team isn't sure if Aurora supports them.

The SRE team has been asked to redesign the architecture to handle flash sales without manual pre-warming. Budget for the redesign is approved but the team must quantify the expected improvement.
questions.question: Which architectural change BEST addresses the recurring flash-sale challenges?
choices: Migrate RDS PostgreSQL to Aurora Serverless v2 (min ACU low, max ACU high) — Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales without manual intervention and handles the 40× variance natively. Verify pg_cron and plpython3u support (both are available on Aurora PostgreSQL). Combine with ECS capacity providers using Fargate + Fargate Spot for cost-optimized scale-out, and DynamoDB on-demand for the catalog/sessions which already scales automatically.
Migrate to Aurora PostgreSQL provisioned with db.r6g.16xlarge instance and add 4 read replicas to always be ready for sales.
Keep RDS PostgreSQL but upgrade to Reserved Instances for the larger db.r6g.8xlarge to reduce always-on cost.
Move order processing to DynamoDB entirely to eliminate the RDS bottleneck.
questions.explanation:
Buried in the scenario are several critical constraints:
- Flash-sale pattern (40× spike, 3-5×/month, 90 min each) — variance is the core problem
- Manual coordination problem (marketing entering times) — automation needed
- Aurora migration concern (pg_cron, plpython3u extensions) — compatibility question
- Budget approved but quantification expected
- Always-on large instance cost concern

Option A (Aurora Serverless v2): this is the answer that addresses every constraint.
- Auto-scaling (0.5–128 ACU range) handles 40× variance natively; no manual pre-warming
- Aurora PostgreSQL DOES support pg_cron and plpython3u (both in the Aurora PostgreSQL extensions list)
- Serverless v2 scales in seconds, not minutes like provisioned
- Cost: pay for capacity used, not peak always-on
- Fargate Spot for ECS cost optimization

Option B (provisioned larger instance + replicas): always-on cost for 3-5 hours/month of actual peak. Wasteful. Also doesn't solve manual pre-warming — provisioned still requires capacity planning.

Option C (RI for larger instance): RI is a financial instrument, not a scaling solution. Still always-on.

Option D (DynamoDB for orders): major refactor; orders typically have relational constraints (transactions, foreign keys to inventory, payments) that DynamoDB models differently.

The pg_cron/plpython3u mention is a red herring — seems like a constraint, but Aurora PostgreSQL supports both. The test is whether you verify compatibility or eliminate Aurora reflexively.

Aurora Serverless v2 is specifically the newer (post-2022) offering that doesn't have v1's significant limitations. v1 had 'pause' behavior, v2 doesn't. v1 had limited Aurora features, v2 has them all.
questions.pattern: Flash-sale architecture: Aurora Serverless v2 for DB auto-scaling, DynamoDB on-demand, Fargate + Fargate Spot for ECS, CloudFront for static. Manual pre-warming is a code smell — the architecture should absorb variance. Aurora Serverless v2 (not v1) is the modern answer; v1 had significant limitations that v2 removed. Always verify extension compatibility but don't eliminate Aurora reflexively.
questions.note: Aurora PostgreSQL supported extensions are listed in AWS docs; both pg_cron and plpython3u are supported. The extension list evolves — always check current docs before assuming a specific extension is or isn't supported.

references.title: ECS capacity providers with Fargate Spot

questions.scenario:
CloudAnalytix is a SaaS provider offering business intelligence dashboards to 2,800 enterprise customers. Their core architecture runs across three AWS regions (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1) with regional isolation — customer data stays in the region where they signed up. The platform uses EKS for compute, Aurora PostgreSQL for metadata, DynamoDB for dashboard state, and S3 for analytical data. Typical customer has 50–500 daily active users querying dashboards.

After 5 years of growth, the CFO is pushing for cost optimization. Current monthly spend: $2.1M across the three regions. Cost Explorer analysis reveals the following breakdown: EC2/EKS compute 38%, RDS 18%, DynamoDB 14%, S3 11%, data transfer 9%, other (CloudFront, NAT Gateways, Lambda, etc.) 10%. The FinOps team flagged that RDS costs grew 43% year-over-year despite customer growth of only 22%. Most of that growth is in Aurora read replicas — currently 3 per region (9 total) added over time to handle dashboard query load.

The engineering team proposed three optimization initiatives: (1) migrate Aurora reads to Aurora Optimized Reads or larger instance types with Graviton processors; (2) implement query-result caching in ElastiCache Redis to reduce database load; (3) adopt Aurora zero-ETL integration with Redshift for analytical queries, offloading complex aggregations from Aurora to Redshift.

The CTO wants to prioritize based on expected cost impact AND implementation risk. Each initiative requires different effort levels and has different risk profiles. The FinOps team needs recommendations within two weeks to present to the board.
questions.question: Which prioritization BEST balances cost impact and implementation risk?
choices: Start with (2) ElastiCache query caching — lowest risk, immediate impact on DB load, reduces need for read replicas. Then (1) Graviton migration for remaining RDS workload. Evaluate (3) zero-ETL to Redshift only if analytical workload justifies Redshift cost; for dashboard queries, Aurora with caching may be sufficient.
Start with (3) zero-ETL to Redshift — moves the most expensive queries to cheaper analytical storage.
Start with (1) Graviton migration — lowest code change, 20% price-performance improvement.
Implement all three simultaneously to maximize savings in the shortest time.
questions.explanation:
This question tests optimization sequencing judgment. The noise: customer count, year-over-year growth, multiple regions, spending breakdown percentages. The real question is ordering initiatives by risk-adjusted value.

Cost analysis:
- RDS is 18% of $2.1M = ~$378k/month
- 43% growth YoY on RDS vs 22% on customers = excess growth driven by read replicas
- 9 read replicas total = significant caching candidate

Option 2 (ElastiCache caching):
- Relatively low risk: caching is opt-in per query, cache misses still work
- Can reduce read replica count if caching covers common queries
- Direct impact on the fastest-growing cost

Option 1 (Graviton):
- Lower risk (instance-type migration), but ~20% savings on RDS = ~$75k/month
- Doesn't address the growth driver (too many read replicas)

Option 3 (Aurora zero-ETL to Redshift):
- Higher risk: requires query rewriting, new service introduction
- Redshift adds its own cost; may not be net savings unless workload fits Redshift's model
- Best value if analytical workload is clearly analytical (aggregations, scans); not necessarily for dashboard queries that may be point-reads or small aggregations

Option A sequence addresses the growth driver first (caching), then optimizes remaining capacity (Graviton). The conditional on Redshift is correct — zero-ETL is powerful but only if workload actually fits Redshift.

Option B (Redshift first): highest risk, biggest architectural change. Not wrong long-term but bad first step.

Option C (Graviton first): lower risk but smaller impact; doesn't address the read-replica proliferation.

Option D (all simultaneously): violates risk management; concurrent changes make debugging harder.

The subtle exam value: cost optimization always sequences by highest-impact-lowest-risk. Caching is classic low-risk high-impact. Architectural rewrites (like Redshift) are high-impact high-risk and should come later with clearer ROI signals.
questions.pattern: Cost optimization sequencing: (1) eliminate waste (orphans, idle), (2) rightsize existing resources, (3) add caching / architectural leverage, (4) major architectural rewrites only if justified. Always prefer low-risk high-impact first — gives time for measurement and course correction before committing to bigger changes. Redshift zero-ETL is powerful but only for workloads that genuinely fit analytical queries.

questions.scenario:
ThornBridge Manufacturing operates 12 industrial facilities across North America, Europe, and Asia. Each facility runs on-premises OT (operational technology) systems controlling manufacturing equipment, plus IT systems for scheduling, inventory, and quality management. The company has been migrating IT workloads to AWS over 3 years, but OT systems remain entirely on-premises for latency and safety-certification reasons.

A new initiative requires near-real-time integration between OT and cloud IT systems: equipment sensor data (from OT) must feed machine learning models in AWS that predict maintenance needs, and production schedule updates (from cloud IT systems) must flow to OT scheduling systems within 30 seconds. Each facility has different OT systems (Rockwell, Siemens, Mitsubishi, GE — dictated by equipment vendor and historical choices), using a mix of OPC UA, MQTT, and proprietary protocols.

Network connectivity between facilities and AWS varies: three major facilities have dual 1 Gbps Direct Connect; six facilities have single 500 Mbps Direct Connect; three smaller facilities use VPN over commodity internet (typically 100-200 Mbps, variable latency). All facilities require that any connectivity outage cannot disrupt OT operations — OT must continue running even if the cloud connection is down for hours.

The CISO has specified security requirements: all OT-to-cloud traffic must be encrypted, authenticated per-device, and auditable. No OT device may be directly internet-accessible. The CTO is evaluating three approaches: (1) AWS IoT Greengrass at each facility running local processing + cloud sync; (2) AWS Outposts at each facility providing AWS services locally; (3) a custom edge computing solution built on EC2-compatible hardware with bidirectional sync to AWS.

Budget is constrained — the smaller facilities cannot justify high-capital-cost solutions.
questions.question: Which approach BEST handles the heterogeneous facility requirements?
choices: AWS Outposts at all 12 facilities to provide consistent AWS services locally.

AWS IoT Greengrass at each facility: runs local (so OT operations continue during connectivity loss), supports OPC UA, MQTT, and custom protocols via Greengrass components, provides local ML inference for predictive maintenance with cloud model updates, handles per-device authentication via IoT certificates, and works across the heterogeneous network tiers (DX and VPN). For smaller facilities, Greengrass runs on modest hardware — unlike Outposts which requires capital-heavy infrastructure.
Custom edge solution on EC2-compatible hardware — maximum flexibility.
Deploy AWS Local Zones at the three larger facilities and use direct VPN from smaller facilities.
questions.explanation:
The noise in this scenario is substantial: 12 facilities, multiple OT vendors, various protocols, network tier differences, CISO requirements, budget constraints. The real decision is 'which edge architecture.'

Option A (Outposts everywhere): Outposts is capital-intensive hardware that requires space, power, cooling, and significant upfront cost (~$250k+ per rack). Three smaller facilities cannot justify this. Consistent AWS experience, but cost-prohibitive at scale.

Option B (Greengrass): addresses every constraint:
- Offline capability (OT continues during connectivity loss) — Greengrass runs local Lambdas, stores data locally, syncs when connected
- Protocol support — Greengrass components for OPC UA, MQTT; custom components for proprietary protocols
- Local ML inference — Greengrass ML components support on-device inference
- Authentication — IoT certificates per device
- Hardware flexibility — runs on modest industrial PCs, not special hardware
- Works across DX and VPN tiers — no special network requirements

Option C (custom edge): reinvents Greengrass. More work, more risk.

Option D (Local Zones): Local Zones are AWS-owned infrastructure in specific metros — they're not deployable at customer facilities. This option misunderstands Local Zones.

The subtle exam value: knowing the difference between Outposts (customer-site AWS hardware), Local Zones (AWS-owned edge infrastructure in metros), Wavelength (5G edge), and IoT Greengrass (software edge runtime). Each fits different scenarios. For heterogeneous customer facilities with OT integration, Greengrass is the right tool.
questions.pattern: AWS edge service selection: Outposts (customer-site hardware, expensive, consistent AWS), Local Zones (AWS-owned, specific metros), Wavelength (5G/mobile), IoT Greengrass (software runtime on customer hardware). For industrial/OT integration with connectivity resilience, Greengrass fits. Outposts is overkill for most IoT scenarios — capital cost doesn't justify for telemetry/control use cases.

questions.scenario:
StreamVault is a video-on-demand platform with 12 million subscribers globally. Their content library includes 35,000 titles across multiple languages and formats, totaling about 8 petabytes of encoded video. The platform uses a hub-and-spoke content distribution model: master content lands in S3 in us-east-1, then is replicated to regional S3 buckets in us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1, and sa-east-1. CloudFront distributes to end users globally.

The business recently won a rights deal with a major European broadcaster requiring that all EU subscriber viewing data, including viewing sessions and analytics events, remain in EU jurisdictions. Viewing data is currently collected by a Kinesis Data Streams pipeline in us-east-1 from edge Lambda@Edge functions attached to CloudFront. The data feeds a Kinesis Data Firehose to S3 for analytics and a DynamoDB global table for real-time recommendation updates.

The VP of Engineering tasked her team to re-architect EU data handling without disrupting the current architecture for other regions. Specific requirements: (1) EU subscriber viewing events must be collected in EU infrastructure and never transit non-EU regions; (2) recommendation updates for EU subscribers must happen within 2 seconds of viewing events; (3) analytics queries must be able to combine EU and non-EU data for global reporting while respecting that EU RAW data doesn't leave the EU (aggregated statistics can); (4) the content library replication to EU S3 buckets must continue.

The team proposed using CloudFront Functions (instead of Lambda@Edge) in EU edge locations to route EU viewing events to EU-region Kinesis, and maintaining the existing us-east-1 pipeline for non-EU events. A concern was raised that CloudFront edge locations aren't strictly EU even when serving EU users — some traffic may hit a CloudFront POP in a non-EU country (e.g., Morocco, which is not EU).
questions.question: Which architecture BEST handles EU data residency requirements?
choices: Use CloudFront Functions at all edge locations to identify EU subscriber IPs via GeoIP and route accordingly. EU events go to a Kinesis stream in eu-west-1; non-EU events go to the existing us-east-1 stream.

Use Lambda@Edge in the REGIONAL edge caches (which are in specific AWS regions, not edge POPs) to process events after initial CloudFront handling. Regional edge caches in the EU (eu-west-1, eu-central-1) process EU events and route to EU Kinesis; regional edge caches outside EU handle others. However, the PRIMARY control is at the application layer: the player/client sends events directly to a regional endpoint based on subscriber region stored in the user profile — API Gateway in eu-west-1 for EU subscribers, not edge-based routing. DynamoDB global tables become region-restricted (not global) for EU data.
Disable Lambda@Edge entirely; use regional API Gateway endpoints in each region with client-side region selection.
Continue with us-east-1 collection; apply Macie to detect and delete EU data after the fact.
questions.explanation:
This question tests deep understanding of CloudFront's architecture and data residency constraints.

CloudFront has two types of infrastructure:
- **Edge locations (POPs)**: 600+ locations globally, including in non-EU countries near EU markets (Morocco, Turkey, etc.). Lambda@Edge runs here in viewer-request/response or origin-request/response triggers.
- **Regional edge caches**: ~13 larger caches in specific AWS regions. Lambda@Edge CAN run here for origin-facing triggers.

The edge-POP-in-Morocco concern is real. If an EU subscriber's request hits a Moroccan POP, and data is processed there, data has transited non-EU territory.

Option A (CloudFront Functions + GeoIP): CloudFront Functions run in all edge locations, which includes non-EU POPs. GeoIP is a viewer-level signal, but the FUNCTION EXECUTION is in whatever POP the request hit. Data residency fails at the execution location.

Option B: the correct answer is nuanced and multi-layered:
- Application-layer control is primary: the client/player knows the subscriber's region (from profile) and sends events to a regional endpoint directly (API Gateway in eu-west-1 for EU subscribers). This bypasses CloudFront's edge layer for analytics events.
- Lambda@Edge in regional edge caches (which ARE in specific regions) is a backup option but less clean than direct application-layer routing.
- DynamoDB global tables become a problem: if the table replicates globally, EU data would flow to non-EU regions. Solution: separate EU-only table, not globally replicated.

Option C (disable Lambda@Edge entirely): partially correct direction (client-side routing), but throws away valid uses of Lambda@Edge for other purposes.

Option D (Macie after the fact): reactive detection doesn't meet 'never transit' residency requirement.

The subtle exam value: testing whether you know CloudFront edge POPs are GLOBAL (not region-bound), that data residency at the edge layer is hard to guarantee, and that application-layer routing via regional endpoints is often the correct architectural answer for strict residency. Also testing DynamoDB global tables caveat — 'global' means data everywhere, incompatible with residency.
questions.pattern: Data residency at the edge: CloudFront edge POPs are not region-bound — Lambda@Edge at POPs runs in whatever POP handled the request, including non-EU POPs. For strict residency, application-layer routing via regional endpoints (API Gateway in specific regions) is cleaner than edge-layer logic. DynamoDB global tables replicate globally — incompatible with 'data stays in region X.' Use regional tables or isolated tables per residency zone.

references.title: CloudFront edge locations vs regional edge caches

questions.scenario:
Carbon Valley Pharmaceuticals is a drug research company with 4,200 employees across labs in Boston, San Diego, Basel, and Singapore. They were FDA-audited in 2024 and received observations about access control and audit trail gaps. The remediation plan includes comprehensive IAM overhaul.

The current state: 147 AWS accounts in one Organization, federated via SAML from Okta (using IAM Identity Center). Most accounts use a baseline permission set called 'Researcher' that grants broad access to S3, SageMaker, EC2, and some other services. Data scientists need access to specific S3 buckets containing clinical trial data, with different buckets having different access controls (some require specific training, some are restricted to specific nationalities for export control, some are time-limited for specific studies).

The new IAM architect proposed an elaborate design using IAM Identity Center permission sets plus attribute-based access control (ABAC) using session tags from Okta. The design has session tags for: training_completed (comma-separated list of trainings), nationality (ISO country code), active_studies (comma-separated list), and department. S3 bucket policies would use conditions matching these session tags against bucket tags (aws:PrincipalTag/training_completed includes 'GCP' AND aws:ResourceTag/required_training, etc.).

The security lead is concerned about complexity — 'we have 2,400 S3 buckets, each with different tags. Writing bucket policies to match session tags for all combinations seems fragile.' The IAM architect insists ABAC is the AWS best practice. The VP of Engineering asks whether there's a simpler approach that still meets FDA requirements.
questions.question: Which approach BEST meets FDA audit requirements with manageable complexity?
choices: Adopt the ABAC design — it's the AWS-recommended pattern for scaling permissions across many resources.

Use AWS Lake Formation for the clinical trial data (which is fundamentally a data-lake pattern with fine-grained access needs). Lake Formation provides tag-based access control purpose-built for this scenario, with tag inheritance, catalog integration, and audit via CloudTrail. IAM Identity Center permission sets handle infrastructure access; Lake Formation handles data access. This separates concerns: IAM Identity Center for services/APIs, Lake Formation for data.
Create one IAM role per study and per training combination, manually maintaining the matrix.
Use OpenID Connect federation directly without IAM Identity Center for simpler permission management.
questions.explanation:
This scenario wraps a data-access problem in an IAM architecture question. The noise: FDA audit context, multi-site organization, elaborate ABAC proposal, architect-vs-security-lead dynamics.

The real insight: for fine-grained access to DATA (S3 buckets with complex conditions on training, nationality, study membership), Lake Formation is AWS's purpose-built tool. It provides:
- LF-Tags (tag-based access control) designed for this scale
- Tag inheritance down data hierarchies
- Integration with Glue Data Catalog
- Audit via CloudTrail
- Cross-account sharing via RAM

Option A (ABAC everywhere): ABAC can work but at 2,400 buckets with multiple dimensions, the policy matrix becomes unmaintainable. Complex condition expressions are fragile and hard to audit.

Option B (Lake Formation for data + Identity Center for services): separation of concerns. Infrastructure access (EC2, SageMaker, Lambda) via Identity Center permission sets. Data access via Lake Formation permissions. Clean, auditable, purpose-built.

Option C (role-per-study): explodes in a matrix; operational disaster.

Option D (OIDC without Identity Center): rebuilds what Identity Center already provides; doesn't solve the data-access complexity.

The subtle exam value: recognizing that the problem is data access, not infrastructure access, and that Lake Formation exists for this specific use case. Many architects default to 'IAM can do everything' and miss Lake Formation's role. FDA audits care about auditable access control — Lake Formation's access grants are CloudTrail-logged and auditable out of the box.
questions.pattern: IAM Identity Center vs Lake Formation: Identity Center handles human-to-AWS-service access (APIs, console). Lake Formation handles human-to-data access (databases, tables, columns, tag-based). For fine-grained data access scenarios, Lake Formation is the right tool. Don't try to encode 2000+ data permissions in IAM policies — use the purpose-built service.

references.title: AWS Lake Formation — tag-based access control (LF-TBAC)

questions.scenario:
Vertex Analytics runs a petabyte-scale data platform on AWS processing advertising analytics for major brands. The architecture is heavily Apache Spark-based, running on Amazon EMR clusters. Typical workload: 40-80 Spark jobs daily, ranging from 15-minute quick analyses to 8-hour batch aggregations. Data lives in S3 (Parquet and Iceberg formats). The team has been running EMR for 4 years on long-running clusters — a large production cluster (50 r5.4xlarge instances) plus a smaller dev cluster (10 r5.2xlarge).

Recent observations: the production cluster's CPU averages 28% utilization across the month but spikes to 90% during peak hours. The dev cluster averages 12% utilization. Monthly EMR cost is $180,000. The platform team has been asked to reduce cost by 40% while maintaining or improving job completion times.

Three alternatives have been proposed:
(1) Migrate to EMR Serverless — pay for only the compute used during each job execution, scale automatically.
(2) Adopt EMR on EKS — run Spark on existing EKS clusters, sharing resources with other workloads.
(3) Keep EMR but switch to transient clusters (create per-job, terminate after) with aggressive Spot instance use and fleet-based provisioning across multiple instance types.

The data engineering lead prefers (1) for operational simplicity. The platform engineering lead prefers (2) for unified infrastructure. The FinOps lead prefers (3) for maximum cost control. The CTO must decide.
questions.question: Which migration BEST balances the 40% cost reduction target with job performance?
choices: EMR Serverless (option 1) — simplest migration, scales to zero between jobs, no cluster management. Pricing model (pay for memory-seconds and vCPU-seconds) aligns with variable workload and typically achieves 40%+ savings on bursty workloads. Job startup is sub-minute for warm pools. Minimal operational overhead. Some workload caveats (strict initialization time, certain Spark configurations) may require minor adjustment.
EMR on EKS (option 2) — shared infrastructure maximizes utilization.
Transient EMR clusters (option 3) — finest-grained control.
Stay on EMR with reserved instances to achieve savings via commitments.
questions.explanation:
The scenario noise: company background, Spark adoption, specific cluster sizes, utilization numbers, advocate preferences. The real question: which gives 40% savings while maintaining performance?

Cost analysis:
- Production 28% average utilization = 72% idle. Long-running cluster pays for idle capacity.
- Dev 12% average = 88% idle.
- Together, ~80% of spend is for idle capacity.

Option 1 (EMR Serverless): pay per vCPU-second and memory-GB-second used. No idle charge. For 28% average utilization, you'd pay for ~28% of current capacity + some overhead = ~30-40% of current cost. 40%+ savings achievable.
- Operational simplicity: no cluster sizing, no idle management
- Job startup: sub-minute with pre-initialized capacity pools
- Caveat: not all EMR workloads run identically; some Spark configurations need adjustment

Option 2 (EMR on EKS): good for organizations with existing EKS and desire for unified platform. But introduces EKS operational complexity to the data team. Savings come from shared-resource efficiency, but depend heavily on what else runs on EKS and whether idle time is truly shared.

Option 3 (Transient + Spot): maximum control, biggest savings potential, but operational complexity is high. Every job needs cluster creation/termination, Spot interruption handling, and cluster-fleet configuration. For 40-80 jobs daily, this is meaningful ops burden.

Option 4 (RIs on EMR): RIs lock capacity at discount, but don't solve the fundamental 'paying for idle' problem. Saves some money, not 40%.

The subtle exam value: EMR Serverless is the 2022+ offering that removes cluster management entirely. For teams without EKS skills and with bursty workloads, it's often the right first move. EMR on EKS is powerful for teams already EKS-deep. Transient EMR is powerful but operationally complex.
questions.pattern: EMR cost optimization: EMR Serverless (pay-per-use, no cluster management) for bursty or low-utilization workloads. EMR on EKS for teams already on EKS wanting consolidation. Transient EMR for steady-state workloads with good Spot tolerance. For 40% savings on 28%-utilized clusters, Serverless typically wins with least operational disruption.

questions.scenario:
Haldenmark Bank is a regional commercial bank with 680 branches across Germany. The IT estate includes a core banking system running on IBM Power systems on-premises (10-year-old hardware), plus satellite systems on Windows and Linux. The Power system hardware reaches end-of-life in 14 months. The CIO has been given a mandate: migrate off Power to a cloud architecture.

The core banking application was written in PL/I and C, with approximately 4 million lines of code. It runs on AIX on Power, with a DB2 database (8 TB, heavily tuned with bufferpool and storage group configurations) and a complex batch architecture with 300+ JCL-equivalent job definitions. Integration with satellite systems uses IBM MQ. Transaction volume is 12 million per day with peaks of 400 TPS during morning openings.

The migration team assessed three paths:
(1) **Rehost** to AWS using a combination of EC2 (for AIX workloads — specifically Dedicated Hosts with custom AIX images if available, though AIX on EC2 has limitations) or Mainframe Modernization with Micro Focus.
(2) **Refactor** using AWS Mainframe Modernization Blu Age to convert PL/I/C to Java running on EC2 or containers.
(3) **Repurchase** — replace the core banking system with a modern SaaS core banking platform (Temenos, Mambu, etc.) and migrate data.

Regulatory context: BaFin (German financial regulator) requires that banks provide evidence of appropriate testing and risk management for core system changes; regulatory approval takes 4-6 months for major core banking changes. The board requires a functioning core banking system in 14 months.

The CTO noted: 'Each migration path has different risk profiles. Getting this wrong is existential — a bank that can't process transactions is a bank that loses its license.'
questions.question: Given the 14-month deadline and regulatory constraints, which path BEST balances risk and feasibility?
choices: **Refactor** via Mainframe Modernization Blu Age — modernized Java code positions the bank for future agility. Estimated 10-12 months for conversion plus regulatory approval.
**Repurchase** — move to modern SaaS core banking, eliminating the Power migration problem entirely while modernizing.

**Rehost** via AWS Mainframe Modernization with Micro Focus runtime running the existing PL/I/C code on AWS-hosted runtime on EC2 (or EC2 with AIX-compatible alternatives). Estimated 6-9 months plus regulatory approval. Refactor can occur in a subsequent phase once stability is established.
Extend the Power hardware lease for 24 months and plan a proper multi-year migration.
questions.explanation:
This is a life-or-death-of-the-business question under deadline. The scenario noise: bank size, branch count, specific technology details, regulatory mention. The real constraints: 14-month hard deadline, regulatory review taking 4-6 months, and 'existential risk.'

Option A (Refactor via Blu Age): conversion timeline 10-12 months + 4-6 months regulatory = 14-18 months. Very tight; if conversion slips (common), the bank misses the deadline.

Option B (Repurchase SaaS): moving to new core banking platform is a multi-year project. Data migration alone for 4 million LOC of custom banking logic + DB2 data would take 12+ months. Adds implementation partner coordination. Too risky for 14 months.

Option C (Rehost via Micro Focus): 6-9 months implementation + 4-6 months regulatory = 10-15 months. Buffer for slippage. Keeps existing code, reducing business logic risk. Modernization happens in phase 2 when not under existential deadline.

Option D (extend hardware): the scenario implies this isn't possible or is undesirable — 'mandate' to migrate. Also depends on Power hardware vendor, which may not offer extension.

The hard tradeoff: Option A produces better long-term artifacts (modern Java) but at higher schedule risk. Option C produces uglier long-term artifacts (COBOL-like code running on a runtime emulator) but is schedule-safer. For existential deadlines in regulated industries, schedule safety wins.

The regulatory point is critical: 4-6 months of approval testing is fixed overhead. Shorter implementation = more buffer before regulatory submission + more buffer after for any regulator-requested changes.

The subtle exam value: testing whether you pick pragmatic Rehost over aspirational Refactor when existence depends on schedule. Mainframe Modernization (M2) supports both runtimes — Micro Focus for rehost, Blu Age for refactor — and the choice depends on schedule risk tolerance.
questions.pattern: Mainframe/legacy migrations with deadlines: rehost first (Micro Focus), refactor later. Regulatory approval adds fixed overhead (4-6 months for banking/healthcare). Repurchase (SaaS replacement) is multi-year — not a short-timeline option. When deadline risk is existential, schedule-safe wins over architecturally-cleaner.
questions.note: AIX on EC2 is not a standard offering — scenarios involving AIX typically require either Power Systems on-premises, a third-party provider (Skytap on AWS historically provided IBM Power in cloud), or migration to another OS. Mainframe Modernization covers IBM z/OS but 'AIX Power' is a different migration pattern. Check current AWS offerings for Power migration paths.

references.title: Mainframe Modernization — Micro Focus vs Blu Age

questions.scenario:
Kerlund Insurance operates a multi-account AWS environment via AWS Organizations with 47 accounts across 4 OUs: Production, PreProd, Development, Sandbox. A central Platform account hosts shared infrastructure including VPCs, Transit Gateway, and recently an API Gateway-based 'customer lookup' service that 18 application accounts need to call.

The platform team wants application accounts to call the customer lookup API without crossing the public internet. They're evaluating two approaches: (A) expose the API Gateway as a Private API and share the execute-api VPC endpoint via RAM, letting each application VPC create its own interface endpoint, or (B) share the Platform VPC's subnets via RAM to the application accounts, and have application workloads run in the shared subnets where they can reach the Private API directly.

The network team also mentions that two of the 18 application accounts belong to a recently-acquired subsidiary that is NOT yet part of Kerlund's AWS Organization. Data residency is eu-west-1 only.
questions.question: Which approach BEST satisfies the requirements?
choices: Share the Platform VPC's subnets via RAM to all 18 application accounts. Applications run in shared subnets and reach the Private API directly. This is simpler operationally than managing multiple VPC endpoints.

Use AWS PrivateLink: expose the API Gateway behind an NLB in the Platform VPC, and create Interface VPC Endpoints in each application VPC pointing to the NLB-based endpoint service. Share the endpoint service via resource-based policy, not RAM. This works both for organization members AND the subsidiary accounts outside the organization. Private API Gateway sharing via RAM is also possible within the Organization but doesn't cross org boundaries for the subsidiary.
Share the Private API via RAM to the 18 accounts including the subsidiary accounts.
Use VPC peering from the Platform VPC to each of the 18 application VPCs.
questions.explanation:
Multiple gotchas compound in this scenario:

**Gotcha 1 — RAM scope**: RAM can share resources to accounts in your Organization (with trusted access enabled) OR to individual accounts via invitation. But some resources have RESTRICTIONS on external (non-org) sharing. The subsidiary accounts being outside the Organization changes what's possible.

**Gotcha 2 — Subnet sharing mechanics**: sharing subnets via RAM (A) puts workloads IN the Platform VPC's subnets. This creates tight coupling: participants can't modify the subnet, route tables, NACLs. For 18 accounts, this is operationally fragile — any network change impacts all consumers. Also, the subsidiary accounts outside the Org cannot receive subnet shares at all (subnet sharing via RAM requires Organizations).

**Gotcha 3 — Private API sharing**: API Gateway Private APIs are accessed via interface VPC endpoints for the execute-api service. You don't 'share' the API via RAM directly — you control access via resource policies on the API and endpoint policies.

**The right architecture**: use AWS PrivateLink (NLB + endpoint service + interface endpoints in consumer VPCs). This works across organization boundaries (subsidiary accounts can consume), scales to many consumers, and keeps network isolation clean. You can use a Private API Gateway behind the NLB OR expose the API via the NLB directly. This pattern is standard for multi-account API sharing at scale.

Option A is operationally problematic (and breaks for the subsidiary). Option C misunderstands what RAM shares. Option D (peering) creates a mesh that doesn't scale and still doesn't cross the org boundary cleanly.
questions.pattern: Cross-account/cross-org API sharing: PrivateLink (endpoint service + interface endpoints) for scale and cross-org support. RAM for within-org sharing of specific resource types (TGW, subnets, Route 53 resolver rules, etc.). Don't conflate 'share an API' with 'share a VPC resource' — different AWS mechanisms. Subsidiary/external accounts force PrivateLink or cross-account IAM patterns.

questions.scenario:
Pacifica Bank operates AWS Organizations with trusted access enabled for AWS RAM. Their network team established a shared-services VPC in a 'Network' account, with Transit Gateway sharing via RAM at the OU level. A new compliance mandate requires that any resource shared from the Network account must be shared ONLY with specific accounts in the Production OU — not entire OUs, not the Development OU, not the Sandbox OU, even by accident.

The Organization administrator proposes using an SCP attached to the Network account that denies 'ram:CreateResourceShare' and 'ram:AssociateResourceShare' unless the target principals match a specific list of Production account IDs. The security lead asks whether this will actually work given that RAM share targets include OUs, accounts, and IAM principals.
questions.question: Which statement about controlling RAM sharing targets via SCPs is CORRECT?
choices: SCPs cannot restrict RAM sharing targets at all because RAM is a cross-account service.

SCPs can restrict RAM actions, but 'principal' in RAM is identified differently depending on share target type (account ID, OU ID, organization ID, IAM role/user ARN). An SCP using condition keys like 'ram:Principal' can restrict allowed principals, but careful policy design is needed — and you also need to consider that ram:AssociateResourceShare is distinct from ram:CreateResourceShare, and that accepting shares is handled by the consumer account (not the sharer's SCP).
RAM sharing is controlled exclusively by IAM, not SCPs.
Use resource-based policies on each RAM share instead of SCPs.
questions.explanation:
RAM + SCP governance has multiple subtleties:

**Gotcha 1**: SCPs set maximum permissions for accounts. They CAN restrict RAM actions — but you need to target the right actions. 'CreateResourceShare' creates a share; 'AssociateResourceShare' adds principals/resources to an existing share. If you only block Create, an existing share can still be modified to add new principals.

**Gotcha 2 — principal types in RAM**: targets can be AWS account IDs, OU IDs, the root of the organization, or IAM role/user ARNs. Your SCP conditions must handle the principal formats you want to restrict. The 'ram:Principal' condition key operates on the principal ARNs added to a share.

**Gotcha 3 — acceptance boundary**: the consumer account has to ACCEPT many types of shares (unless auto-accept is enabled for within-Org shares). Your SCP on the sharing account doesn't control what the consumer does.

**Gotcha 4 — RAM vs shared resource**: RAM shares a resource handle, but the underlying resource behavior (e.g., TGW attachment, subnet usage) may have its own access controls. An SCP restricting RAM share targets doesn't inherently restrict what consumers do WITH shared resources.

Option B is the honest answer: yes SCPs work, but careful design is needed across all these dimensions.
questions.pattern: RAM governance via SCPs: target ram:CreateResourceShare AND ram:AssociateResourceShare (both). Use ram:Principal condition key with appropriate principal format matching. Consider auto-accept settings for within-Org shares. Layer with Service Control Policies at OU level to catch the sharing account's operations. Don't forget that shared resources have their own permission models.

questions.scenario:
Vancor Aerospace has 120 AWS accounts across 8 OUs. Central IT manages a shared-services account with Route 53 Resolver rules for forwarding DNS queries to on-premises resolvers (for legacy internal domain lookups like 'corp.vancor.internal'). These rules are currently shared via RAM to all application accounts.

A new request comes in: a specific set of 12 accounts (part of a new classified-projects OU) must NOT receive these shared resolver rules — they should use different on-premises resolvers for security isolation. The network architect proposes two options:

**Option A**: Exclude the classified-projects OU from the existing resource share and create a separate resource share for those 12 accounts with different resolver rules.

**Option B**: Keep the existing share scoped to the root of the Organization, but apply an SCP to the classified-projects OU that denies access to Route 53 Resolver APIs entirely, forcing those accounts to set up their own resolvers from scratch.

The security lead adds that the classified accounts should still be able to use AWS-provided DNS (AmazonProvidedDNS) for general AWS service resolution, just not for the corp.vancor.internal domain.
questions.question: Which approach BEST meets the requirement with manageable operations?
choices: Option B — deny all Route 53 Resolver APIs via SCP for classified accounts; they'll build their own from scratch.

Option A — scope existing shares to EXCLUDE the classified OU and create a targeted share with appropriate resolver rules for those accounts. RAM resource shares can be associated with specific OUs or accounts, so you can restructure which principals receive which shares. Existing application code and DNS resolution continue to work; only the backing resolver rules differ per OU.
Share both sets of resolver rules to all accounts and let each account pick which to use.
Use Route 53 Private Hosted Zones exclusively instead of Resolver rules.
questions.explanation:
**Gotcha 1 — scoping shares**: RAM resource shares have an 'associated principals' list that can include OUs, accounts, the Org root, or specific IAM principals. You can SCOPE a share by creating multiple shares with different principal sets. Re-scope the existing share to Org root EXCEPT the classified OU (by associating individual OUs other than classified), then create a new share for classified with different rules.

**Gotcha 2 — Option B's problem**: denying all Route 53 Resolver APIs means classified accounts can't even query AWS-provided DNS for AWS service resolution (S3, DynamoDB, etc. endpoints rely on DNS). That breaks virtually everything. SCPs at this granularity need careful condition design to allow AWS service DNS while blocking shared-rule usage.

**Gotcha 3 — Option A detail**: resolver rules are shared as RAM resources. When a rule is shared to an account, it's associated with VPCs in that account. Removing a share disassociates the rule — traffic for that domain falls back to default resolution. Creating a NEW share with different rules for the classified accounts routes 'corp.vancor.internal' to their alternate on-prem resolvers.

**Gotcha 4 — RAM and OUs**: resource shares can be associated with OUs. Trusted access with Organizations must be enabled. Auto-acceptance applies within the Organization.

Option A is operationally clean and achieves the security isolation. Option B causes collateral damage to AWS service resolution.
questions.pattern: RAM share scoping at OU granularity: multiple shares with different principal associations. Route 53 Resolver rules shared via RAM = per-domain routing control that can be scoped. For security isolation between OUs, don't reach for SCPs that deny broad APIs — scope the underlying resource shares instead. SCPs are blunt; RAM targeting is precise.

questions.scenario:
Astora Media operates 60 AWS accounts for various media production workloads. A shared-services account hosts a subnet-sharing architecture via RAM: 4 subnets (2 public, 2 private, across 2 AZs) are shared with 38 'content production' accounts. Workloads in participant accounts launch EC2 instances, ECS tasks, and Lambda functions (VPC-enabled) into the shared subnets.

During a compliance review, the FinOps team raises cost concerns: participants are running unlimited NAT Gateway traffic (charged to the shared-services account), and there's no way to attribute costs back to individual participants. The security team separately raises that each participant can see all other participants' ENIs in the shared subnets via DescribeNetworkInterfaces, which leaks information about other tenants.

The architecture team is considering three changes: (1) deploy per-account NAT Gateways in each participant VPC (requires moving away from shared subnets), (2) implement VPC endpoint policies to control who can use which NAT, (3) use Cost Allocation Tags on ENIs and have participants tag their resources for chargeback.
questions.question: Which statement about the shared-subnet architecture is CORRECT?
choices: Shared subnets mean shared cost and blast radius by design. Participants can describe ENIs in shared subnets (this is documented behavior, not a security bug). NAT Gateway data transfer charges accrue to the OWNER account (shared-services), and there's no native AWS mechanism to attribute them per-participant. The architecture team must choose: accept these properties, or migrate to per-account VPCs with Transit Gateway-based connectivity (using RAM to share the TGW) for isolation and per-account attribution.
Enable VPC endpoint policies to prevent participants from seeing other ENIs.
Cost Allocation Tags automatically attribute shared NAT Gateway traffic by source ENI owner.
Shared subnets provide complete isolation between participants.
questions.explanation:
Shared subnets via RAM have specific documented properties that this scenario surfaces:

**Gotcha 1 — cost attribution**: NAT Gateway data processing charges accrue to the account that OWNS the NAT Gateway (the shared-services account). There's no mechanism to attribute this per-source-ENI. Cost Allocation Tags don't help here — they apply to resources, not to the traffic through shared infrastructure.

**Gotcha 2 — ENI visibility**: participants can describe ENIs in shared subnets. They CAN'T modify ENIs they don't own, but they can see that ENIs exist, their IP addresses, and some metadata. This is documented behavior. VPC endpoint policies don't affect this — it's an EC2 API level concern.

**Gotcha 3 — blast radius**: a misbehaving participant can exhaust IP addresses in shared subnets, affecting other participants. Subnet CIDR changes require careful coordination. Route table changes aren't allowed by participants.

**Gotcha 4 — the real trade-off**: shared subnets are GREAT for a small number of trusted workloads in one team. They're problematic at 38 accounts with mixed trust levels and cost concerns. The pattern that scales: per-account VPCs + Transit Gateway (sharable via RAM) for network interconnection. Each account owns its VPC (isolation, per-account NAT costs, no ENI leakage), while TGW provides connectivity.

Option A is the honest technical answer. The architecture has hit the limits of the shared-subnet model.
questions.pattern: Shared subnet anti-patterns: (1) large number of participants, (2) mixed trust levels, (3) per-participant cost attribution needed, (4) participant-level network autonomy needed. When these emerge, migrate to per-account VPC + RAM-shared Transit Gateway model. Shared subnets work best for small teams / small account counts with same trust boundary.

questions.scenario:
Stratheim Pharmaceuticals operates a dedicated 'Security' account in their AWS Organization for centralized GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail organization trail, and now a new requirement: a centralized API Gateway-based 'policy evaluation' service that all 240 application accounts must call during deployments to check compliance. The API must be callable from CI/CD pipelines running in any application account.

The platform team implements a Regional API Gateway with IAM authorization (AWS_IAM method). They configure the API's resource policy to allow invocation from specific IAM roles in application accounts — but listing 240 account IDs in the policy is hitting resource policy size limits, and each new account requires a policy update.

The team is evaluating scalable alternatives:
- **Option A**: Use 'aws:PrincipalOrgID' condition in the API resource policy to allow any role in the Organization
- **Option B**: Switch to a Lambda authorizer that looks up allowed accounts in a DynamoDB table
- **Option C**: Move to Cognito User Pool with SAML federation
- **Option D**: Share the API via RAM to application accounts
questions.question: Which option scales BEST while keeping governance manageable?
choices: Option B — Lambda authorizer with DynamoDB lookup, most flexible.

Option A — use aws:PrincipalOrgID condition in the resource policy. The policy grants invocation to any IAM principal in the Organization, eliminating the need to enumerate accounts. New accounts work automatically. IAM authorization + SigV4 signing still provides the auth flow. Combine with additional conditions (aws:PrincipalTag, source VPC, etc.) for finer control.
Option C — Cognito for machine-to-machine auth.
Option D — RAM share the API Gateway.
questions.explanation:
**Gotcha 1 — RAM doesn't share API Gateway**: API Gateway isn't in the list of RAM-shareable resources. You can't 'share' an API via RAM. Authentication to API Gateway uses its own mechanisms (IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizers, API keys). Option D is incorrect at the premise level.

**Gotcha 2 — aws:PrincipalOrgID**: this IAM global condition key matches the AWS Organization ID of the calling principal. Using it in the API's resource policy means 'any IAM role in my Organization can invoke.' Scales to any account count; self-maintaining as accounts are added/removed. The standard idiomatic pattern for organization-wide API access with IAM auth.
